We find that ideology-contested items are consistently harder than non-contested ones, and that across 18 of 20 models, accuracy is systematically higher when the empirically verified causal sign aligns with intervention-oriented expectations than with market-oriented ones. Moreover, when models err, their incorrect predictions disproportionately lean intervention-oriented, and this directional skew is not eliminated by one-shot in-context prompting. These results highlight that LLMs are not only less accurate on ideologically contested economic questions, but systematically less reliable in one ideological direction than the other, underscoring the need for direction-aware evaluation in high-stakes economic and policy settings.
author: Donggyu Lee, Hyeok Yun, Jungwon Kim, Junsik Min, Sungwon Park, Sangyoon Park, Jihee Kim

title: Recursive Multi-Agent Systems
content:
arXiv:2604.25917v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Recursive or looped language models have recently emerged as a new scaling axis by iteratively refining the same model computation over latent states to deepen reasoning. We extend such scaling principle from a single model to multi-agent systems, and ask: Can agent collaboration itself be scaled through recursion? To this end, we introduce RecursiveMAS, a recursive multi-agent framework that casts the entire system as a unified latent-space recursive computation. RecursiveMAS connects heterogeneous agents as a collaboration loop through the lightweight RecursiveLink module, enabling in-distribution latent thoughts generation and cross-agent latent state transfer. To optimize our framework, we develop an inner-outer loop learning algorithm for iterative whole-system co-optimization through shared gradient-based credit assignment across recursion rounds. Theoretical analyses of runtime complexity and learning dynamics establish that RecursiveMAS is more efficient than standard text-based MAS and maintains stable gradients during recursive training. Empirically, we instantiate RecursiveMAS under 4 representative agent collaboration patterns and evaluate across 9 benchmarks spanning mathematics, science, medicine, search, and code generation. In comparison with advanced single/multi-agent and recursive computation baselines, RecursiveMAS consistently delivers an average accuracy improvement of 8.3%, together with 1.2$\times$-2.4$\times$ end-to-end inference speedup, and 34.6%-75.6% token usage reduction. Code and Data are provided in https://recursivemas.github.io.
author: Jiaru Zou, Rui Pan, Ruizhong Qiu, Pan Lu, Shizhe Diao, Jindong Jiang, Hanghang Tong, Tong Zhang, Markus J. Buehler, Jingrui He, James Zou

title: Let It Be Simple: One-Step Action Generation for Vision-Language-Action Models
content:
arXiv:2606.05737v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Generating diverse images from sparse text is hard; generating compact actions from rich observations is easier. From the condition-target view, Vision-Language-Action (VLA) thus aligns with image-to-text, not text-to-image. We formalize this view through the irreducible velocity loss $R_v(t,c)$ of standard flow matching and validate it with a controlled 8-mode toy experiment and image-to-text MNIST task. We then show that high-noise training boosts one-step VLA decoding on standard LIBERO, achieving 95.6% on LIBERO-Long, and remains competitive across LIBERO-Plus, LIBERO-Pro, and real-world robot tasks, while ablations that weaken the condition or expand the horizon predictably erase the one-step gain. These results suggest that whether one-step action generation works in VLA depends not on specialized training, but on the condition-target structure.
author: Yitong Chen, Shiduo Zhang, Jingjing Gong, Xipeng Qiu

title: CoCo-SCR: Covisibility-Centric Scene Coordinate Regression for Large-Scale UAV Visual Localization
content: Visual localization is a fundamental capability for uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) remote sensing, where the goal is to estimate the camera pose of a query image in a known scene. In large-scale UAV scenarios, this task remains challenging due to severe viewpoint variation, complex flight trajectories, and extensive low-texture regions. Scene coordinate regression (SCR) provides a compact learning-based localization paradigm by directly predicting scene coordinates from image observations. These challenges weaken the geometric supervision used in SCR and hinder reliable cross-view implicit triangulation. Existing SCR methods do not model covisibility sufficiently. In particular, single-view reprojection supervision lacks an explicit bias toward genuinely covisible regions, while global descriptors optimized mainly for appearance similarity often provide unreliable priors for covisibility. To address this limitation, we propose CoCo-SCR, a covisibility-centric training framework in large-scale UAV visual localization. Specifically, we introduce an explicit covisible matching prior (ECMP) to strengthen local geometric supervision in truly covisible regions, and global covisibility encoding (GCE) to learn covisibility-aware global descriptors. Unlike feature-matching-based localization, these matching and overlap cues are used only to reorganize training supervision and do not introduce explicit 3-D model storage or local descriptor matching during inference. Experimental results on public datasets demonstrate that CoCo-SCR consistently improves localization accuracy in large-scale UAV scenes while maintaining single-model inference and a compact map representation.
author: Ling Zheng, Wenhao Lin, Kan Ren, Qian Chen

title: Unifying Exponential and Gaussian: Whittle–Matérn Covariance for Improved Rough Surface Characterization and Backscatter Modeling
content:
Monitoring soil moisture using synthetic aperture radar (SAR) data is an important technique due to its capability of providing consistent, high spatial resolution, and all-weather observations. However, its accuracy is often limited by simplified representations of surface roughness in physical scattering models. Commonly used exponential and Gaussian correlation functions impose fixed structural assumptions that may not adequately capture multiscale terrain characteristics. This article adopts the Whittle–Matérn (WM) covariance as a unified and flexible roughness representation for microwave surface scattering models. By continuously bridging exponential and Gaussian forms through the smoothness parameter  $
u $ , the WM model enables consistent characterization of surface structures ranging from single-scale to multiscale regimes. To support its practical integration into advanced scattering formulations, we develop a numerically stable mixed-scale approach for computing higher order spectral densities with  $\mathcal {O}(n)$  computational complexity, together with a second-order asymptotic expansion for large  $
u $ . Validation using published farmland measurements demonstrates that the WM model provides a flexible statistical representation compared with canonical correlation functions. Its integration into advanced physical surface scattering models such as the mEAIEM enables the role of the smoothness parameter  $
u $  to be explicitly examined in the modulation of the backscattering coefficient  $\sigma ^{0}$ . Comparisons with measured backscattering data and analytical reference solutions confirm physically consistent behavior under realistic surface conditions. The proposed framework provides a statistically consistent extension of conventional scattering models, supporting their application across SAR systems operating at different spatial resolutions, including ALOS-2, RADARSAT-2, and Sentinel-1.
author: Yang Du, Yiqi Wang, Zhou Shi, Zhaoliang Li

title: Efficient Incremental Large-Scale Orthophoto Generation via 3-D Gaussian Splatting SLAM
content: This article presents a scalable and stable 3-D Gaussian splatting (3DGS)-simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) framework for efficient large-scale orthophoto generation. Unlike conventional SfM- or SLAM-based pipelines that rely on geometry-driven stitching, we reformulate orthophoto generation as a rendering-based mapping problem under a unified 3DGS-SLAM paradigm. However, directly applying 3DGS-SLAM to aerial mapping suffers from critical challenges, including uncontrolled memory growth, optimization instability, and slow convergence in large-scale UAV scenarios. To address these issues, we introduce a unified framework that jointly enforces memory-constrained representation, stabilized optimization, and accelerated convergence during incremental mapping. Specifically, we design an online gradient-driven mechanism to regulate Gaussian evolution, a viscous velocity regularization to stabilize optimization dynamics, and a geometry-aware homography-guided densification strategy to accelerate convergence under planar scene priors. Furthermore, by aligning GNSS with the SLAM system, our framework enables globally consistent orthographic rendering, producing geometrically and geographically consistent orthophotos in a unified coordinate frame. Extensive experiments on multisource UAV datasets, including those equipped with ground control points (GCPs), demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high absolute metric accuracy, along with superior efficiency, stability, and visual fidelity, enabling practical real-time incremental orthophoto generation for large-scale aerial environments.
author: Xiao Zhang, Hongbin Dong, Xiaozhou Zhu, Haoxin Zhang, Shuaixin Li, Baosong Deng

title: Assessment of Ionospheric Delay Corrections: Insights From Jason-3 and Sentinel-6A Tandem Phases
content: Dual-frequency altimeter measurements can be used to extract information on the ionospheric delay correction in the atmosphere and the total electron content below the satellites. Precise knowledge of the stability and accuracy of these corrections is essential to judge their impact on sea level studies and for ionospheric investigations. This article assesses the uncertainties of satellite altimetry ionospheric corrections from Jason-3 and Sentinel-6A by analyzing their data from two tandem phases (TPs). The mean offset of the ionospheric correction between both missions is 3 mm with a small dependence on significant wave height (SWH), so that the difference can be up to about 1 cm for larger sea states. Sentinel-6A shows greater along-track noise and some residual impacts of undetected rain events. While retracking and version changes have a small effect on the mission differences, the applied smoothing approach has a more significant influence. The smoothing in the current GDR-G version of Jason-3 seems to be too strong and leads to an underestimation of about 5% of the largest corrections within the equatorial ionization anomalies.
author: Denise Dettmering, Christian Schwatke

title: Quantifying Interpretability of Res-U-Net and U-Net Performance in Identifying Ocean Fronts Based on Thermal Infrared Remotely Sensed Data
content: Accurate identification of ocean fronts is vital for understanding marine ecosystems, fisheries, and biogeochemical processes. Although deep learning models such as U-Net and its residual-enhanced variant, Res-U-Net, have improved front segmentation, their black-box nature limits interpretability and credibility. To address this, we integrated Grad-CAM++, an interpretability technique that generates class-specific attention heatmaps, allowing deeper insight into the internal feature extraction mechanisms. Our study systematically compares the decision-making processes of U-Net and Res-U-Net, focusing on the interpretability and segmentation performance. Res-U-Net incorporates residual blocks to alleviate gradient vanishing and enhance multi-scale feature extraction, enabling better localization of subtle frontal boundaries in complex oceanic conditions. Grad-CAM++ visualizations reveal that Res-U-Net consistently attends to physically meaningful structures, such as sharp thermal gradients, which are characteristic signatures of ocean fronts formed by strong water-mass contrasts, and retains fine spatial features even in deeper layers. For a fair comparison, we selected the best-performing configurations from both model families. Compared to the 21-layer U-Net, the 45-layer Res-U-Net achieved a 19% increase in IoU and a 24% improvement in pixel-level correlation. Qualitative heatmaps further show that Res-U-Net maintains localized attention on front structures, while U-Net exhibits dispersed and blurry focus, highlighting the superior front-resolving capability and interpretability of Res-U-Net.
author: Yi Wang, Yuan Niu, Xuefeng Zhang, Dianjun Zhang

title: KunPeng: A Global Ocean Environmental Model
content: Ocean environmental forecasting is a core component in studying global climate change, disaster warning, and fishery prediction. Although deep learning technologies have provided a new paradigm for ocean modeling, existing ocean-specific models still face challenges such as insufficient capability in representing local features and inadequate prediction accuracy. This study innovatively migrates AI large-model techniques to the ocean domain, constructing the KunPeng global ocean environmental prediction model. To address the discontinuous characteristics of marine space, we propose a terrain-adaptive mask constraint mechanism to effectively mitigate training divergence caused by abrupt gradients at land–sea boundaries. We integrate longitude-cyclic deformable convolution (LC-DCN) to expand the receptive field of dynamic convolution, enabling high-precision modeling of small-scale features in the ocean. A deformable convolution-enhanced multistep prediction (DC-MTP) module is employed to strengthen temporal dependency feature extraction capabilities. Experiments show that compared to other AI-based meteorological large models, this model achieves an average anomaly correlation coefficient (ACC) of 0.801 in 15-day global forecasts at a 0.25° resolution, representing an improvement of 0.01 to 0.08 over the benchmark models; its normalized mean squared error (mse) is 0.404, achieving a relative reduction of 5.3% to 31.7%, and its normalized mean absolute error (MAE) is 0.442, achieving a relative reduction of 0.4%–20.5%. It also demonstrates comprehensive superiority over traditional numerical weather prediction models such as BlueLink, CMCC, Forecasting Ocean Assimilation Model (FOAM), and PSY4. Notably, it exhibits significant improvements in the prediction of sea surface and deep-ocean features as well as flow velocity fields. A horizontal comparison of operator types further shows that local operators consistently outperform global ones under the slow-varying dynamics of the ocean, confirming the effectiveness of a convolution-based dynamic feature pyramid for forecasting ocean physical indicators. The code can be found at https://github.com/kbdsbx/loem
author: Yi Zhao, Jiaqi Li, Haitao Xia, Tianjiao Zhang, Zerong Zeng, Tianyu Ren, Yucheng Zhang, Chao Zhu, Shengtong Xu, Hongchun Yuan

title: CannySegNet: Canny-Guided Deep Learning for Firn Layer Extraction and Thickness Estimation in Airborne Snow Radar Echograms
content: The firn layer serves as a key proxy for understanding the near-surface structure, dynamic processes, and evolutionary history of snow and ice. However, existing methods often treat the firn layer as a single “line” during extraction. Moreover, due to the inherently low SNR of snow radar images and the ambiguity of layer boundaries, the extraction accuracy remains limited. To enhance the precision of layer extraction, this study proposes an automatic firn layer extraction and thickness estimation framework based on semantic segmentation. The core algorithm, CannySegNet, refines boundary details in feature space through a feature fusion module and incorporates structural priors for weight guidance, thereby directing the model’s focus to the correct layers and significantly improving the capability of extracting layer boundaries. The results show gains of 2.35% in mIoU, 0.95% in precision, and 1.86% in  $F1$ -score, while the mean absolute error (MAE) is reduced by 0.04. In the test of nearly a hundred consecutive radar echo images, the consistency rate reached 85.63%. The complete pipeline further incorporates data augmentation, dual-model cooperative inference, and Canny-edge-guided refinement. When applied to multiyear radar data, the method reveals systematic spatial variations in firn layer thickness: thicker layers concentrate along the Antarctic margin and thin progressively toward the interior. In addition, layer thickness is inversely correlated with surface slope, bed elevation, and surface elevation.
author: Hanqiang Zhou, Yang Zhou, Xiaofei Hu, Jiali Tian

title: Coastal Sea Ice Monitoring With GNSS-IR Using the Spectral Area Factor and Multifrequency Bayesian Fusion
content: Under ongoing Arctic warming, robust monitoring of coastal sea ice freeze–thaw dynamics requires indicators that are not only physically interpretable but also quantitatively validated. Although global navigation satellite system interferometric reflectometry (GNSS-IR) is a promising ground-based approach, existing studies primarily focus on qualitative interpretation of time-series patterns, with limited quantitative assessment of metric performance under heterogeneous coastal environments. To address this gap, this study introduces the spectral area factor (SAF), derived from spectral analysis of GNSS signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) data, and systematically quantifies its monitoring capability using a Bayesian classification framework. Using five years (2018–2022) of observations from the Arctic coastal station TUKT in Canada, we estimate class-conditional statistics from 2018 to 2020 and evaluate the performance on independent data from 2021 to 2022. The proposed SAF shows strong discriminative capability between sea ice and sea water compared to conventional feature factors and provides a stable response during transitional periods. A multifrequency Bayesian fusion model using SAF at GPS L1/L2/L5 bands achieves an accuracy of 92.72% for near-real-time arc-level classification and 98.63% for daily-scale post-processing classification. More importantly, the model outputs class probabilities rather than conventional binary labels, allowing it to accommodate the inherent spatial heterogeneity of coastal sea surface states. The resulting probabilistic time series supports near-real-time coastal sea ice state inference with hourly-scale updates and provides quantitative information on local sea ice conditions within the study area. In addition, the framework is operationally flexible, enabling adaptive fusion of available frequency combinations and offering a scalable path for future extension to multiconstellation GNSS observations. Overall, these results demonstrate a practical route from feature design to quantitative, application-oriented coastal sea ice monitoring using existing ground-based GNSS infrastructure.
author: Xintai Yuan, Shengping He, Andreas Brack, Jens Wickert

title: TPFMNet: A Probabilistic U-Net With Triple-Path Fusion Module for Spatially Adaptive Uncertainty in Year-Round Arctic Sea Ice Forecasting
content: Accurate year-round Arctic sea ice concentration (SIC) forecasting with reliable uncertainty quantification is critical for polar navigation and climate risk assessment. While deep learning (DL) has significantly improved predictive accuracy, existing deterministic models often fail to account for the extreme nonstationarity and inherent noise in satellite-derived sea ice products, particularly during extreme seasonal transitions such as the spring predictability barrier (SPB). In this study, we propose TPFMNet, an efficient probabilistic U-Net-based framework designed for spatially adaptive uncertainty estimation in year-round Arctic SIC forecasting. The core of the architecture is the tri-path fusion module (TPFM), which explicitly decouples multiscale physical dynamics into three synergistic pathways: 1) dual-scale neighborhood attention for capturing highly localized kinematic fractures; 2) zero-inference-cost structurally reparameterized convolutions for resolving broad morphological patterns; and 3) a global frequency-domain mixer for tracking basin-scale seasonal periodicities. In addition, a geographically guided coordinate attention mechanism embeds spatial priors to accurately monitor the dynamically sensitive marginal ice zone (MIZ). To quantify heteroscedastic aleatoric uncertainty without the computational burden of ensemble methods, TPFMNet models the predictive distribution as a Laplace distribution, optimized via negative log-likelihood (NLL). Extensive experiments on OSI SAF historical datasets demonstrate that TPFMNet achieves state-of-the-art predictive performance, significantly outperforming recent spatiotemporal and domain-specific baseline models. Specifically, it yields a superior overall mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.01670 and an  $R^{2}$  of 0.9603. The probabilistic outputs exhibit exceptional calibration, maintaining a prediction interval coverage probability of over 95% while generating sharp, informative bounds. Notably, the estimated uncertainty is physically consistent with Arctic cryospheric processes, adaptively intensifying during the highly volatile summer melt season and explicitly mitigating the SPB. These results establish TPFMNet as a robust, highly efficient tool for operational Arctic maritime applications. The code will be made publicly available at: https://github.com/wmingqiang01/TPFMNet
author: Mingqiang Wang, Yanhai Gan, Feng Gao, Junyu Dong

title: Analyzing the Spatio-Temporal Pattern of Disturbances in Yuzhong Mining Areas Using Continuous Variation Detection and Intensive Time-Series Landsat Observation
content: Disturbance detection and restoration monitoring are essential in mining regions for understanding landscape dynamics and supporting restoration decision-making. However, traditional remote sensing approaches often suffer from sparse temporal observations and phenological noise, which limit their capacity to capture subtle or short-term ecological dynamics. To overcome these issues, we develop an enhanced intensive time series LandTrendr (ITSLT) framework based on dense Landsat time series derived from all available surface reflectance imagery from 2000 to 2023, and integrate key temporal breakpoints for improved disturbance extraction. In addition, a dynamic time warping (DTW)-based trajectory similarity analysis is introduced to classify disturbance–recovery patterns and quantify deviation from reference baseline sequences. Applied to the Yuzhong mining area in central Henan, China, the proposed method improves the temporal sensitivity and robustness of disturbance characterization. Results show pronounced spatial heterogeneity in disturbance magnitude and recovery trajectories. Human-induced disturbances account for 52.91% of all events while mining-related impacts contribute 2.61% and 5.98% of the total area, highlighting the dominant role of anthropogenic activities. Although regional ecosystem conditions exhibit an overall recovery trend, they have not yet returned to the levels observed in 2000. Disturbances have progressively expanded from core mining zones toward adjacent construction and transportation corridors, reflecting a shift from localized to more spatially connected anthropogenic impacts. Our combined ITSLT with DTW framework provides an effective tool for long-term disturbance detection and ecological recovery assessment in mining landscapes, and supports application in ecological restoration monitoring and green mine planning.
author: Jiao Pan, Tao Chen, Ling Peng, Antonio Plaza

title: Hyperspectral Estimation of Community-Weighted Mean Traits in Wetland Plant Communities
content: Plant functional traits reflect plant resource-use strategies and responses to environmental stress and, thus, provide an important entry point for understanding wetland ecological processes. However, traditional field-based approaches to measuring functional traits are constrained in spatial coverage and efficiency by the poor accessibility and complex environmental conditions of marsh wetlands. In this study, three typical marsh wetlands within the Crested Ibis National Nature Reserve were selected as study sites. A UAV-borne hyperspectral sensor was used to acquire canopy spectral information, and quadrat surveys were conducted to determine wetland plant species composition. Six functional traits, including leaf area, leaf thickness, plant dry matter content, and leaf carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus contents, were measured and converted into community-weighted mean traits (CWMs). Using vegetation indices and fractional-order differential spectral features, we developed hyperspectral estimation models for the CWMs. The results showed that: 1) fractional differentiation of order 0.50–1.25 substantially enhanced spectral feature extraction; 2) hyperspectral data showed good potential for estimating CWMs in wetlands, with optimal coefficient of determination (R2) values ranging from 0.56 to 0.64; and 3) different CWMs exhibited trait-specific responses to spectral features and models. This study provides a technical foundation for efficient, fine-scale monitoring of wetland plant functional structure and supports ecological conservation and management of wetland ecosystems.
author: Huazhe Li, Yuan Zeng, Lijuan Cui, Xiying Tang, Xiajie Zhai, Xinsheng Zhao, Jinzhi Wang, Yinru Lei, Rumiao Wang, Wei Li

title: Parameter-Efficient Local-Context Cooperation via Vision Foundation Models for UHR Remote Sensing Image Segmentation
content: Ultrahigh resolution (UHR) remote sensing image segmentation aims to achieve a fine-grained understanding of complex ground scenes. In recent years, vision foundation models (VFMs) have shown strong capability in learning generic structural priors from large-scale visual data, indicating great potential for such fine-grained scene understanding. However, their application to UHR remote sensing images remains limited, as the massive parameter scales of VFMs are difficult to train under UHR remote sensing images. Motivated by the success of the parameter-efficient fine-tuning paradigm on VFMs, we propose a novel parameter-efficient local-context cooperation (PEACE) framework, which significantly reduces trainable parameter overhead while improving segmentation accuracy. In particular, PEACE leverages a shared VFM with minimal trainable parameters to collaboratively process local and corresponding contextual patches partitioned from the UHR remote sensing image. A multireceptive local adapter (MRLA) and a multireceptive context adapter (MRCA) are designed to capture spatial features of local and contextual inputs across multiple receptive fields. Finally, contextual semantics are integrated into local representations. Furthermore, a context-sensitive assistance strategy (CSAS) leverages the correct prediction of the context to effectively overcome the primary limitations of the patch-based training paradigm. Experimental results demonstrate that PEACE effectively exploits VFMs and remote sensing foundation models (RSFMs) with minimal parameter increments and achieves versatility and superior performance across several UHR remote sensing image benchmarks.
author: Qi Li, Chunxiao Chen, Jiaxin Cai, Junjie Huang, Yansheng Li, Wenxi Liu

title: Context-Aware Multimodal Representation Learning for Spatio-Temporally Explicit Environmental Modeling
content: Earth observation (EO) foundation models have emerged as an effective approach to derive latent representations of the Earth system from various remote sensing sensors. These models produce embeddings that can be used as analysis-ready datasets, enabling the modeling of ecosystem dynamics without extensive sensor-specific preprocessing. However, existing models typically operate over large spatial or temporal contexts, limiting their use for ecological analyses that require modeling across task-specific spatial scales and diverse temporal conditions. To overcome these limitations, we propose a representation learning framework that integrates different EO modalities into a unified feature space on a high-resolution spatio-temporal grid. We introduce the framework using Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data as representative modalities. Our approach produces spatio-temporally explicit embeddings aligned with the native 10-m spatial grid and the temporal frequency of cloud-free Sentinel-2 acquisitions. Each sensor is first modeled independently to capture its sensor-specific characteristics. Their representations are then combined in a shared model. This two-stage design enables modality-specific optimization and easy extension to new sensors, retaining pretrained encoders while training only the added fusion layers. The fusion model achieved a validation reconstruction MAE of 0.0004 for Sentinel-1 and 0.0039 for Sentinel-2 on normalized inputs, indicating accurate cross-modal reconstruction. Qualitative analyses reveal that the learned embeddings exhibit high spatial and semantic consistency across heterogeneous landscapes. Quantitative evaluation in modeling gross primary production reveals that they encode ecologically meaningful patterns and preserve the temporal frequency required for fine-scale environmental analyses, with an NRMSE of 0.0917. Overall, the proposed framework provides a flexible, analysis-ready representation learning approach for environmental applications requiring diverse spatial and temporal contexts.
author: Julia Peters, Karin Mora, Miguel D. Mahecha, Chaonan Ji, David Montero, Clemens Mosig, Guido Kraemer

title: MSDCNet: A Multilevel Spatial Distribution-Enhanced Coupled Network for Ship Counting in Remote Sensing Images
content: Ships in remote sensing images typically exhibit scale variations and dense distributions, posing significant challenges for accurate counting. To address these issues, we propose a multilevel spatial distribution-enhanced coupling network, which integrates spatial structure modeling and density estimation within a unified framework to fully exploit the distribution characteristics of ships and enhance the perception of different-sized targets. Built upon a pretrained feature extraction module, the network employs a multiscale feature fusion module (MFFM) to enhance contextual modeling and improve spatial response across diverse scales. The segmentation feature-aware module (SFM) leverages point-level supervision and segmentation-aware signals to provide auxiliary spatial structural constraints for density estimation. In terms of optimization, a multicollaborative density loss function is designed to jointly model density regression and boundary constraints, thereby achieving deep coupling between spatial structure information and density estimation. Extensive experiments on three public remote sensing object counting (RSOC) datasets demonstrate that multilevel spatial distribution-enhanced coupled network (MSDCNet) achieves superior performance in terms of mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE). Specifically, MSDCNet obtains MAE/RMSE values of 2.05/3.45 on DIOR-C and 2.58/4.22 on SIMD-C. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed multilevel spatial distribution-enhanced coupled design for dense ship counting in complex remote sensing scenes. Datasets and code are publicly available at https://github.com/Woohoy/MSDCNet
author: Yaxiong Chen, Qijian Li, Qingwang Wang, Guirong Feng

title: SoDa2: Single-Stage Open-Set Domain Adaptation via Decoupled Alignment for Cross-Scene Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Cross-scene hyperspectral image (HSI) classification stands as a fundamental research topic in remote sensing, with extensive applications spanning various fields. Owing to the inclusion of unknown categories in the target domain and the existence of domain shift across different scenes, open-set domain adaptation (OSDA) techniques are commonly employed to address cross-scene HSI classification. However, existing OS cross-scene HSI classification methods still face two critical challenges: 1) domain shift issues arising from the direct alignment of mixed spectral–spatial features and 2) high computational costs caused by two-stage training strategies. To address these issues, this article proposes a single-stage open-set domain adaptation method based on decoupled alignment (SoDa2) for cross-scene HSI classification. A contribution-aware dual-modality feature extraction is customized to disentangle the characteristics from spectral sequence signals and spatial details, selectively and adaptively enhancing discriminative features. The decoupled alignment module minimizes the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to independently reduce the spectral discrepancy and the spatial discrepancy between the source and target domains, extracting more fine-grained domain-invariant features. A cost-effective single-stage dual-branch framework is designed to learn MMD-constrained aligned features and constraint-free intrinsic features for adaptive distinction between known and unknown classes. This framework employs a Gaussian mixture model (GMM) to model the squared cosine similarity distribution between the two feature types, enabling OS recognition without prior knowledge of unknown classes. Extensive experiments on three groups of HSI datasets demonstrate that SoDa2 outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior classification accuracy and model transferability for OS cross-scene tasks. The SoDa2 code will be available at https://github.com/liuyiwen523/SoDa2
author: Yiwen Liu, Minghua Wang, Jing Yao, Xin Zhao, Gemine Vivone

title: A Graph Neural Network-Embedded Surface Energy Balance Method for Temporal Normalization of All-Sky Land Surface Temperature
content: Land surface temperature (LST) is a key variable in the surface energy cycle. However, due to the wide swath width of the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS), local solar time (LS time) differences can reach up to 2 h, resulting in temporal incomparability of LST. Although surface-energy-balance (SEB)-derived methods have been developed for temporal normalization, they overlook the correlation between air temperature and longwave downward radiation (DLR) and exhibit limited applicability under cloudy-sky conditions. To address these challenges, a graph neural network-embedded SEB (GNN-SEB) method was developed for the temporal normalization of all-sky LST. First, a new physical relationship was established using the Bowen ratio (BR), the SEB model, and the Stefan–Boltzmann law. Then, a two-stage SEB-guided GNN was applied to reconstruct missing LST. Finally, an improved SEB (ISEB) method was derived based on first-order Taylor expansions of shortwave downward radiation (DSR) and DLR. Results show that GNN-SEB outperforms the random forest regression (RFR) (3.13–3.59 K), SEB-BR (2.43–3.30 K), and GOT09-dT- $\tau $  (2.42–3.31 K) models, with root mean square error (RMSE) values ranging from 2.41 to 3.28 K across different sites. The improvement increases with LS time differences, particularly between 0.8 and 0.9 h, where the RMSE decreases by 0.94–1.07 K. The method also reduces RMSE compared with MODIS LST across all seasons. Under cloudy-sky conditions, it corrects LST differences caused by temporal inconsistencies between Coordinated Universal Time and the normalized time. By integrating GNN into the SEB framework, the GNN-SEB model achieves temporal normalization of all-sky LST, offering improved transferability, interpretability, and applicability.
author: Zhenwei Wang, Yingbao Yang, Xin Pan, Penghua Hu, Yuncheng Chen, Zi Yang, Xiaoxiao Kong, Yang Dai

title: Sparse Sampling Reconstruction of Wind Fields for Space-Borne Doppler Radars
content: With its conically scanning W-band Doppler radar, the Wind Velocity Radar Nephoscope (WIVERN) ESA Earth Explorer 11 mission promises to provide, for the first time, global vertically resolved wind observations in cloudy and precipitating systems. Thanks to its observation geometry, WIVERN will measure line-of-sight (LoS) Doppler velocities of the same atmospheric volume from different viewing directions, allowing the complete reconstruction of the wind vector. To that end, a methodology to retrieve horizontal wind vectors for a conically scanning radar with a swath of 800 km and sparse sampling is developed in this work. The retrieval is applied to different meteorological systems, whose dynamics are simulated with a high-resolution cloud-resolving model. Results show that, in the presence of a healthy number of cloud targets in the scene, it is possible to reconstruct the zonal and meridional winds with uncertainty within 2–4 m/s. Accuracies deteriorate in the central part of the swath, close to the ground track, due to sparser sampling and interdependencies between different radar looks.
author: Sérgio Luiz E. F. da Silva, Alessandro Battaglia, Cinzia Cambiotti, Anna F. Carbone

title: Multistatic SAR Sequential Imaging Method Using Subaperture Spatial Spectrum Fusion Based on High-Precision Trajectory Reconstruction
content: This article focuses on simultaneously achieving a short synthetic aperture time and high imaging resolution to improve the observation capability of microwave-band synthetic aperture radar (SAR). To this end, multistatic SAR (MSAR) for sequential imaging is investigated. Through the distribution and collaboration of multiple radars, MSAR synthesizes a long azimuth aperture much faster than conventional SAR. However, insufficient positioning accuracy of the MSAR platforms introduces significant errors in imaging and leads to the failure of high-resolution multistatic image formation. A key challenge lies in how to coherently fuse echoes from MSAR under multiplatform trajectory errors. To address this issue, we propose an MSAR sequential imaging method using subaperture spatial spectrum fusion based on high-precision trajectory reconstruction, so as to ensure imaging accuracy. In this method, multiplatform multiorder trajectory errors in MSAR sequential imaging are modeled, and a high-precision multiplatform trajectory reconstruction method is developed based on the quantitative relationship between subaperture image offsets and trajectory error parameters. Subsequently, an MSAR sequential imaging framework is established to compensate for multiplatform trajectory errors and coherently fuse subaperture images from all radars, thereby improving image resolution. For validation, a three-uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) MSAR system is established, and a sequential imaging experiment is conducted. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated using real measurements, demonstrating an enhancement in imaging resolution.
author: Xinyu Mao, Zhongyu Li, Yu Hai, Shen Zhong, Xingchen Ming, Chaodong Wang, Wanmin Wu, Junjie Wu, Jianyu Yang

title: Variance in Attitude Estimates From SAR Doppler Measurements
content: The Doppler centroid measured by a synthetic aperture radar (SAR) as a function of slant range is sensitive to the combined effects of spacecraft (SC) attitude and antenna pointing. In principle, the measured Doppler can be inverted to estimate this combined attitude, and ancillary data such as star tracker and gyroscope measurements can be used to obtain an estimate of SAR antenna pointing. This article derives a closed-form expression for the expected variance of effective attitude estimates based solely on SAR Doppler measurements, representing one aspect of the overall in-orbit SAR pointing calibration.
author: Razi Ahmed, Jan-Willem de Bleser, Scott Hensley

title: Geometry-Enhanced Interference Localization in Bistatic SAR via Virtual Baseline Construction
content: Bistatic synthetic aperture radar (SAR) provides multidimensional Earth observation capability, but radio frequency interference (RFI) can degrade SAR image quality and bistatic interferometric coherence. Compared with monostatic SAR, bistatic SAR provides additional spatial information that can be exploited to infer the position of RFI sources. This article provides the first demonstration and assessment of geometric degeneracy in bistatic SAR configuration and proposes an RFI localization method via virtual-baseline construction on measured bistatic RFI data. First, the bistatic propagation geometry is explicitly modeled, and a hyperbolic intersection localization model is established. The geometric degeneracy mechanism is explained through the derivation and analysis of the geometric dilution of precision (GDOP). Second, a virtual-baseline construction method is introduced to synthesize new observation geometries by pairing RFI pulses across different time indices. Furthermore, an optimal baseline-pair selection strategy based on GDOP assessment is proposed to further improve localization accuracy while mitigating geometric degeneracy. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified using Monte Carlo simulations and measured bistatic SAR data. The results demonstrate the feasibility of RFI localization in bistatic SAR and show that the proposed virtual-baseline strategy improves localization accuracy from 20 km to a tens-of-meter level.
author: Yifei Liu, Mingliang Tao, Yanyang Liu, Junli Chen, Ling Wang

title: Adaptive Model-Free Tsallis Entropy Estimation for Detecting Non-Fully Developed Speckle
content: Detecting departures from the fully developed speckle hypothesis in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is essential for reliable interpretation in remote sensing applications. To address this challenge, this article proposes a statistical test based on a nonparametric estimator of Tsallis entropy, a nonadditive generalization of Shannon entropy that provides enhanced sensitivity to heavy-tailed distributions characteristic of textured SAR data. The estimator incorporates bootstrap correction to improve accuracy with small sample sizes. The test is integrated within an adaptive windowing strategy that locally selects the optimal window size based on regional homogeneity: larger windows in homogeneous regions to stabilize estimation and smaller windows in heterogeneous areas to preserve structural details. This combination yields an unsupervised statistical framework that generates per-pixel  $\boldsymbol {p}$ -value maps for detecting departures from fully developed speckle. Experimental validation using both simulated data and SAR imagery confirms the method’s precision in detecting texture variability and discriminating between homogeneous and heterogeneous regions. The proposed approach offers an interpretable tool for automated SAR image analysis without requiring training data or explicit parametric texture estimation.
author: Janeth Alpala, Alejandro C. Frery, Paolo Gamba, Abraão D. C. Nascimento, Andrea A. Rey

title: MDLM: Mining Deep Subspace Prior With Low-Rank Difference Minimization for Multispectral Pansharpening
content: Panchromatic sharpening (pansharpening) is a promising technology designed to fuse multispectral (MS) images with panchromatic (PAN) images. However, the low correlation between PAN and MS data often results in spectral and spatial inconsistencies, which limit the scalability of optimization-based models over multiple iterations. In this article, we propose a deep subspace (DS) prior-driven framework for MS pansharpening to fully leverage spatial–spectral priors. Unlike existing methods that rely solely on either data-driven or model-driven approaches, our work integrates the deep neural network’s prior knowledge to guide the optimization process of an explicit pansharpening analytical model. Specifically, we propose a novel DS prior to address the problem of overcomplete representation and to maintain spatial–spectral consistency in a lightweight manner. In addition, we introduce a rank difference minimization (RDM) model that incorporates a nonlocal self-similar prior into the spatial fidelity term, effectively reducing interference and improving overall performance. To evaluate the proposed method, we conduct experiments on four mainstream satellite datasets, namely, WorldView-3 (WV3), WorldView-2 (WV2), QuickBird (QB), and IKONOS (IK), and further validate its generalization capability on GaoFen-2 (GF-2) scenes. The results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances the spatial detail quality of fused images while reducing spectral distortion, thereby highlighting its effectiveness and potential for practical applications.
author: Guoxia Xu, Rui Zhang, Tianxiang Dai, Lizhen Deng, Yansheng Li, Hu Zhu

title: UAV Small Object Detection Based on Dual-Domain Feature Fusion and Lightweight Upsampling and Downsampling
content: Small object detection in uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) remote sensing images faces severe challenges, including tiny object size, weak features, and strong background clutter. Single-modality methods depend heavily on lighting and fail in nighttime or complex scenes, while traditional multimodal fusion schemes incur high computational cost and often suffer from the loss of fine-grained features for small object. We propose YOLO-WEH, a multimodal small object detection algorithm with cross-modal feature fusion and lightweight up/downsampling. The dual-branch backbone extracts complementary features from visible (RGB) and infrared (IR) data, and a wavelet-Fourier dual-domain cross self-attention fusion (WFDF) module explicitly preserves high-frequency details and filters thermal noise for semantic alignment. We also introduce a high-frequency enhanced lightweight downsampling (HFE-LDS) module and an enhanced lightweight content-aware reassembly of features (EL-CARAFEs) upsampling module, which recover the geometric and structural boundaries of infinitesimal objects while maintaining computational efficiency. On three public datasets (DroneVehicle, LLVIP, and FLIR), YOLO-WEH achieves mean average precision (mAP) of 74.9%, 97.4%, and 73.1%, using only 5.0 M parameters and 13.3 GFLOPs, outperforming state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods. Inference speeds reach 90.4, 112.4, and 121.5 frames per second (FPS), respectively, demonstrating high accuracy, lightweight design, and real-time capability. Ablation studies and visualizations confirm each core module’s effectiveness and highlight the algorithm’s advantage in reducing missed and false alarms in multisource heterogeneous and extreme scenarios.
author: Zhe Yuan, Wenchao Meng, Baolong Guo, Zhihui Wen, Junbin Zhuang

title: S2FR-Net: Semantic Reliability-Aware Spatial–Frequency Redistribution for High-Fidelity Pansharpening
content: Pansharpening reconstructs high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) images by fusing a high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) image with a low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) image. Existing methods typically inject PAN details according to saliency or feature correlation, without explicitly assessing whether such structures are reliable for transfer to the underlying multispectral (MS) content, which often results in spectral distortion, texture over-injection, and error propagation during global interaction. We propose S2FR-Net, a semantic reliability-aware spatial–frequency redistribution framework that formulates pansharpening as an estimate–propagate–verify process. Specifically, the frequency redistribution module (FRM) estimates local transfer reliability in the wavelet domain by jointly modeling semantic preference, directional allocation, detail deficiency, and structural compatibility, enabling selective high-frequency redistribution. The semantic decision propagation (SDP) module converts local reliability into an explicit spatial–spectral policy and propagates it through lightweight gated global interaction for globally coherent fusion. The cross-modal context modeling (CCM) module further verifies propagated responses against PAN structure and local fusion evidence to suppress structurally unsupported enhancement before reconstruction. Experiments on WorldView-II, QuickBird, and Maryland show that S2FR-Net achieves competitive or favorable performance under the adopted reduced-resolution and full-resolution protocols. Cross-dataset and perturbation experiments further suggest improved transferability and robustness under the tested settings, while the efficiency analysis indicates a favorable performance-complexity tradeoff. https://github.com/Rui0913/S2FR-Net.git
author: Rui Hao, Xin Jin, Hongyue Huang, Keqin Li, Cheng Xie, Qian Jiang

title: FMSE-DGAF: Frequency-Modulated Spectral Enhancement and Dynamic Global–Local Attention Fusion Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification is a challenging task owing to high dimensionality, spectral redundancy, limited labeled samples, and the presence of mixed pixels. Although models based on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), Transformers, and hybrid architectures have improved feature extraction capabilities, they remain limited in simultaneously capturing complementary frequency information, fine-grained local details, and long-range global context. To address these challenges, this article proposes the frequency-modulated spectral enhancement and dynamic global–local attention fusion (FMSE-DGAF) method, which integrates three key modules: a frequency-modulated spectral enhancement module (FMSEM), a global–local feature fusion module (GLFFM), and a dynamic weighted convolutional attention module (DWCAM). First, the FMSEM strengthens discriminative spectral structures by modulating frequency-domain amplitudes while preserving phase information. Second, the GLFFM integrates global contextual information with fine-grained local spatial structures, stabilizing intermediate spatial representations and effectively aligning coarse-grained semantic cues with fine-grained spatial representations. Finally, the DWCAM combines local convolutional enhancement with global self-attention to achieve multiscale and multigranularity spectral–spatial feature fusion, while emphasizing informative features and suppressing redundancies. Comprehensive evaluations on five benchmark HSI datasets with a wide spatial resolution range (0.109–30 m, approximately a 300-fold variation) show that FMSE-DGAF delivers consistently competitive classification performance, attaining overall accuracies of 99.89%, 99.96%, 99.91%, 99.89% and 99.76% on the Pavia University, Salinas, WHU-Hi-LongKou, WHU-Hi-HanChuan, and Botswana datasets, respectively. These results verify the effectiveness, robustness, and strong generalization capability of the proposed method for HSI classification tasks.
author: Kun Hu, Xiaoqing Wan, Hui Liu

title: Sim2Real-HAD: A Simulation-Driven End-to-End Hyperspectral Anomaly Detection Framework
content: To address the lack of end-to-end learning capability and the scarcity of real-world data in existing hyperspectral anomaly detection (HAD) methods, which limit model performance, this article proposes a simulation-driven end-to-end HAD framework. First, a background–anomaly simulation pipeline tailored for anomaly detection (AD) is developed to automatically generate hyperspectral images with diverse backgrounds and complex distribution characteristics, providing scalable large-scale training data for model learning. Second, an end-to-end HAD network is designed based on the simulated data, enabling a direct mapping from raw hyperspectral observations to pixel-level AD results, thereby jointly optimizing feature extraction and anomaly discrimination. Furthermore, Reed–Xiaoli (RX) residual constraints, anomaly sparsity constraints, and spectral similarity constraints (SSCs) are embedded into the training process to enhance anomaly discrimination capability and cross-scene generalization performance. Extensive experiments conducted on 113 public hyperspectral scenes demonstrate the superior detection accuracy and generalization ability of the proposed method. The code will be available at https://github.com/fomanwong/Sim2Real
author: Kewen Huang, Jie Wang, Ke Li, Yiping Xu, Yan Tian

title: Spatio-Temporally Inconsistent Hyperspectral Image Superresolution via Category-Guided Diffusion With Scale-Co-Occurrence Assistance
content: Fusion-based hyperspectral image superresolution (HSI-SR) methods have achieved strong performance. However, these methods typically assume that the high-resolution multispectral image (HR-MSI) and low-resolution hyperspectral image (LR-HSI) are captured over the same scene at the same time, which is often impractical. Leveraging spatio-temporally inconsistent HR-MSI to assist LR-HSI in HSI-SR is significant for breaking this constraint. HSI-SR based on such unpaired images faces two challenges: identifying structurally similar texture details from unpaired images without spatial correspondences while coping with insufficient reference information; reliably aggregating spatial details from the reference image while preserving spectral fidelity. To address these challenges, we propose a scale-co-occurrence assisted category-guided matching and aggregation diffusion network (C2MA-Diff-Net), built upon a conditional diffusion framework to perform matching and aggregation for unpaired HSI-SR. In particular, during the reverse denoising process, we design a novel matching and aggregation block that integrates a self-scale-co-occurrence assisted category-guided patch matching module (C2PM) and a spectral–spatial corrected aggregation module (S2CA) to jointly perform patch matching and feature aggregation, progressively refining the noisy latent. The C2PM module performs category-guided progressive patch matching (CGPM) with the assistance of self-scale-co-occurrence, efficiently identifying texture-similar patches from HR-MSI while mining cross-scale complementary information from LR-HSI itself. The S2CA module applies center-aware sliding-window spectral–spatial attention to correct reference features and selectively aggregates global information via adaptive-gated Cross-Mamba, enabling reliable aggregation while preserving spectral fidelity. Experimental results show that C2MA-Diff-Net delivers superior performance on unpaired HSI-SR, confirming the effectiveness of our method. Code is available at https://github.com/Jiahuiqu/C2MA-Diff
author: Bo Zhu, Wenqian Dong, Jiahui Qu, Nan Li, Yunsong Li

title: Spectral–Spatial–Frequency Geometry Adaptive Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: The key to hyperspectral image (HSI) classification lies in the effective integration of spatial and spectral features. Furthermore, existing deep learning methods primarily operate in the spatial and spectral domains, often overlooking the potential of the frequency domain to capture global structural patterns and edge details. Because of this, a novel spectral–spatial–frequency geometric adaptive network (SFGANet) is proposed here. Specifically, a dual-domain feature modulator (DDFM) is introduced, which enhances the representation of details in the spatial domain through spatial–channel joint calibration while dynamically modulating components in the frequency domain to suppress noise and enhance task-relevant global geometric structures and edge textures, ultimately achieving the complementary integration of fine spatial features and global frequency features. Concurrently, a spectral band gating module (SBGM) is designed to enhance discriminative spectral features. To achieve deep synergy and complementarity of spatial and spectral branching features, an adaptive geometric interaction block (AGIB) is developed, which consists of geometry semantic synergistic attention (GSSA) and a feedforward network (FFN) with geometric enhancement capabilities. This architecture achieves a learnable, adaptive balance between cross-branch semantic interaction and local structure refinement. Furthermore, it employs a tokenization strategy to compress the high-dimensional features of the two branches into compact semantic tokens for classification prediction. In addition, the architecture effectively fuses the classification predictions from both branches using dynamic decision fusion (DDF), thereby mitigating the issue of overfitting with few labeled samples. Experiments were conducted on four datasets, and the results demonstrate that the proposed SFGANet significantly outperforms existing classification methods, thereby proving the model’s effectiveness and superiority in HSI classification with limited training data.
author: Boxun Dun, Danfeng Liu, Liguo Wang, Jón Atli Benediktsson

title: A Radiometric and Color Balancing Method for Aerial Imagery Based on Remote Sensing Radiometric References
content: Large-scale aerial imagery color consistency and fidelity are critical for accurate interpretation and analysis in applications such as surveying and mapping, ecological monitoring, and disaster assessment. However, existing techniques face two primary challenges: detail loss and local color casts in single-image correction due to the lack of a physical radiation model, and residual discrepancies in multi-image global optimization caused by insufficient radiometric references. To address these issues, this study proposes a radiometric and color balancing method for aerial imagery based on remote sensing radiometric references. The framework first constructs a physical model for nonuniform color compensation using radiometric control points (RCPs), effectively mitigating detail loss and local casts in individual images. Subsequently, radiometric constraints are integrated into the aerotriangulation framework to ensure global consistency across multiple images. Comprehensive evaluations on three representative aerial datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms established approaches, including Wallis, Retinex, hybrid histogram matching (HHM), LORACCS, DCC, C4RSD, and RS-NormGAN. Experimental results indicate significant improvements, particularly in complex scenarios with uneven illumination. Compared to the top-performing benchmark, the proposed method reduced color distance (CD) and DeltaE by 36.36% and 28.91%, respectively, on the DMC dataset. For the DMZ1 dataset, which exhibits severe color casts, CD and DeltaE decreased by 8.06% and 28.32%, while peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) increased by 0.21 dB. In the DMZ2 dataset, characterized by large illumination differences, mean squared error (mse) and DeltaE were reduced by 8.77% and 9.09%, respectively, with a PSNR improvement of 0.37 dB. Furthermore, the method excelled in preserving radiometric fidelity and texture details, offering a robust solution for high-quality remote sensing product generation.
author: Yuting Qin, Zhiling Yu, Yansong Duan

title: Visible-to-Infrared Image Translation via Disentangled Imaging Attribute Transfer
content: Visible (VI)-to-infrared (IR) image translation aims to synthesize IR images from easily accessible VI acquisitions, offering a cost-effective alternative to expensive IR data collection in remote sensing scenarios. Despite remarkable advances achieved, existing methods still struggle to generate high-fidelity IR images. This limitation stems from their reliance on holistic image-level domain-to-domain mapping, which prioritizes global visual alignment while failing to bridge the gap between the imaging attributes of the two modalities. To tackle this critical issue, we propose a novel disentangled architecture for VI-to-IR image translation, which abandons direct global domain mapping and instead explicitly models the transfer of thermal imaging attributes. In particular, we first decompose the latent representation of each image into two disentangled components: modality-invariant semantic content and modality-specific imaging attributes. Subsequently, an attribute transfer module is designed to map VI-specific imaging attributes to the IR domain. Finally, the transferred IR attributes and preserved semantic content are fed into a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) to synthesize IR images with authentic thermal properties. Furthermore, we adopt a multistage end-to-end training paradigm integrated with dedicated hybrid loss functions to ensure stable model convergence and enhance the fidelity of the generated IR images. Extensive experiments conducted on the AVIID, Day-DroneVehicle, and Night-DroneVehicle benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms multiple state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations. The source code is available at https://github.com/silver-hzh/VIAT
author: Zonghao Han, Zixiang Ye, Fangqi Shi, Zhengtao Chen, Kun Hu, Shaohui Mei

title: Adaptation, Modeling, and Calibration: Rethinking Remote Sensing Change Detection With a Dual-Stream Framework
content: Remote sensing change detection (RSCD) reveals surface dynamics through the comparison of bitemporal images, providing critical support for land use supervision, ecological degradation assessment, and disaster emergency response. However, existing methods exhibit significant limitations in complex scenarios when addressing challenges such as cross-scale correlation, local feature aggregation, and ambiguous region calibration, highlighting an urgent need to optimize the RSCD technical paradigm. This study proposes a robust framework (AMCCD) based on dual-stream FastSAM and heterogeneous module cascading, enhancing the model’s change detection capability through a hierarchical optimization paradigm encompassing adaptation, modeling, and calibration. First, a symmetric dual-stream network is constructed using parameter-frozen FastSAM to accurately extract multiscale and high-semantic features from bi-temporal images, which preserves the pretrained domain-invariant semantic representation capability. Second, a heterogeneous mixture of experts (HMoE) module is designed, which adaptively activates heterogeneous expert units via a dynamic routing mechanism to achieve precise matching and efficient fusion of change features across different scales, with solid theoretical justification for the expert combination design tailored for RSCD tasks. Meanwhile, a superpixel segmentation-guided Mamba (SSGM) module is proposed to convert 2-D images into spatially contiguous superpixel node sequences. Leveraging Mamba’s advantage of linear complexity, this module efficiently captures long-range dependencies of irregular patch-like changes. Finally, a cascaded uncertainty calibration (CUC) module is built to generate learnable uncertainty masks, guiding Mamba to perform bidirectional sequential feature propagation and suppressing pseudo-change annotations as well as missed detections. Comprehensive experiments on five challenging RSCD datasets (Levir-CD, WHU-CD, S2Looking, CLCD, and SYSU-CD) demonstrate that the proposed AMCCD method achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a maximum mIoU of 92.07% on Levir-CD and 78.20% on CLCD. Systematic ablation studies, loss function comparative experiments, and zero-shot cross-dataset generalization tests further validate the rationality of each core module and the overall robustness as well as cross-domain transferability of the framework. The code is available at https://github.com/baiboat/AMCCD-Net
author: Wenchao Zhang, Wenhui Ye, Zhenhua Yu, Ruowei Gui

title: PEDNet: Prior-Guided Multielliptical Frequency Masking and Dual-Domain Fusion for Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation
content: Semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing imagery (RSIs) remains challenging due to the coexistence of direction-sensitive structures, complex land-cover patterns, and insufficient class discriminability. Existing methods often emphasize either spatial-domain local details or global contextual aggregation, while paying limited attention to the anisotropic frequency characteristics induced by real ground objects, often resulting in the suboptimal learned representations. To address this issue, we propose a prior-guided multielliptical frequency masking and dual-domain fusion network (PEDNet), a unified prior-guided anisotropic spatial–frequency modeling framework for remote sensing semantic segmentation (RSSS). The core idea of PEDNet is to align frequency modeling with the directional geometry and semantic heterogeneity of land-cover objects, rather than relying on fixed isotropic decompositions. Specifically, a multielliptical frequency decomposition module (MEFDM) is introduced to perform anisotropy-aware frequency partitioning in the Fourier domain using learnable elliptical masks, thereby enhancing anisotropic spectral modeling and boundary-detail perception. In addition, a contextualized dual-attention fusion module (CDAFM) and a prior-guided mask adaptation module (PGMAM) are developed to realize coordinated spatial–frequency interaction and scene-adaptive frequency modeling, where CDAFM strengthens cross-domain feature integration through contextualized bidirectional attention and gated fusion, and PGMAM dynamically refines frequency partitioning under semantic prior guidance. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that PEDNet consistently achieves superior segmentation performance, especially in challenging scenarios involving fine boundaries, anisotropic structures, and densely distributed small objects.
author: Pengfei Huang, Ke Zhang, Hongmei Wang, Jingyu Wang

title: SAM-Guided Semantic and Motion Changed Region Mining for Remote Sensing Change Captioning
content: Remote sensing change captioning (RS-CC) is an emerging and popular research task that aims to describe, in natural language, the content of interest that has changed between two remote sensing images captured at different times. Existing methods typically employ CNNs/Transformers to extract visual representations from the given images or incorporate auxiliary tasks to enhance the final results, with weak region awareness and limited temporal alignment. To address these issues, this article explores the use of the segment anything model (SAM) foundation model to extract region-level representations and inject region-of-interest knowledge into the captioning framework. Specifically, we employ a CNN/Transformer model to extract global-level vision features, leverage the SAM foundation model to delineate semantic- and motion-level change regions, and utilize a specially constructed knowledge graph (KG) to provide information about objects of interest. These heterogeneous sources of information are then fused via cross-attention, and a Transformer decoder is used to generate the final natural language description of the observed changes. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance across multiple widely used benchmark datasets, especially in terms of BLEU-4 and CIDEr-D scores. The source code of this article has been released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/SAM_ChangeCaptioning
author: Futian Wang, Mengqi Wang, Xiao Wang, Haowen Wang, Jin Tang

title: Surface Seismic Constraint-Based Suppression of Migration Artifacts in VSP Imaging
content: The acquisition geometry of vertical seismic profiling (VSP) inherently leads to heterogeneous spatial sampling of subsurface reflectors. In particular, fold coverage decreases rapidly with increasing distance from the receiver well, producing severe artifacts in migrated images. Surface seismic data, which are commonly acquired simultaneously in VSP survey areas, are structurally consistent with the VSP imaging targets. Because the dips associated with migration artifacts differ substantially from those of true geological structures imaged by surface-seismic data, these surface-seismic structural attributes can provide robust prior constraints. This article proposes two new migration artifact attenuation methods that exploit such prior information. The first method, termed the dip-angle-difference constraint, compares dip differences between VSP and surface-seismic images; VSP amplitudes are preferentially preserved within a prescribed dip-confidence interval by means of a Gaussian decay weight. The second method is dip-guided Laplacian filtering, which constructs an anisotropic filter from surface-seismic structure tensors and iteratively smooths the VSP image along geologically consistent directions. Reverse time migration (RTM) experiments on synthetic and field data show that both methods effectively suppress migration artifacts, whereas dip-guided Laplacian filtering provides higher computational efficiency and better preservation of structural continuity. The proposed approach therefore offers a robust postprocessing tool for high-precision VSP imaging.
author: Chongyang Han, Hui Zhou, Weixin Yang, Yong Zeng, Yu Wang, Shanbo Sheng, Qin Su

title: Plug-and-Play Regularized Multidimensional Deconvolution for Accurate Wavefield Retrieval and Imaging
content: Surface-related multiples remain an obstacle to accurate subsurface imaging across many acquisition settings. Multidimensional deconvolution (MDD) provides an effective framework for retrieving reflection responses while removing surface-related multiples and the source signature. However, practical MDD inversion can be sensitive to acquisition limitations, incomplete illumination, and noise contamination. To improve the stability and accuracy of MDD, we reformulate it within a plug-and-play (PnP) regularization framework. In this approach, an implicit prior, implemented through a denoiser acting as a proximal operator, guides the inversion process. This enables the use of advanced denoisers to suppress incoherent noise while preserving geologically meaningful events, leading to cleaner wavefield retrieval and a more reliable reflection response. Test on a synthetic dataset constructed from drill-bit noise shows that the proposed approach yields cleaner reflection responses and fewer imaging artifacts than standard MDD. Application to the Volve field data further demonstrates the robustness and practical value of the method, indicating its potential for stable and high-fidelity seismic imaging.
author: Ning Wang, Tariq Alkhalifah

title: Corrections to “A New Model for Elevation Change Estimation in Antarctica From Photon-Counting ICESat-2 Altimetric Data”
content: In the above article [1], we correct the following texts that contain inaccurate descriptions and numerical errors: (1) Page 6 (Section III B): In the fourth paragraph of the left column the last sentence “...... $\sigma_{\frac{dh}{dt}_{Grid} }$ , is obtained through an error propagation from ......” is revised to “...... $\sigma_{\frac{dh}{dt}_{Grid} }$ , is estimated as the root mean square of ...... .”(2)Page 9 (Section IV D): In the last paragraph of the left column: the sentence “...... volumetric losses in the basin, $-81.9~\pm$ 0.8 km3 yr-1......, with an increase of $-14.5 \pm 1$ km3 yr-1” is revised to “...... volumetric losses in the basin, $-81.9~\pm$ 3.5 km3 yr-1......, with an increase of $-14.5~\pm$ 3.6 km3 yr-1.”(3)Page 12 (Section Appendices C): In the third paragraph of the right column the sentence “......, which are mostly under 0.5 cm yr-1” is revised to “......, which are mostly under 1.0 cm yr-1.”(4)Page 12 (Figure 15): The figure contained numerical errors and is revised. The black area in the lower-left corner represents the open ocean region.
author: Rongxing Li, Youquan He, Hongwei Li, Yuanyuan Gu, Guojun Li, Gang Qiao, Huan Xie, Tong Hao, Xiangbin Cui

title: Cross-Domain Few-Shot Infrared Ship Segmentation With Class-Specific Adapters and SAM Refinement
content: Infrared (IR) ship semantic segmentation is pivotal for all-weather maritime surveillance and national early-warning applications. However, the significant domain gap between IR and visible images, combined with the extreme scarcity of annotated samples in the target IR domain, poses a dual challenge that severely restricts the cross-domain application of segmentation networks. Existing few-shot segmentation methods mostly assume similar data distributions, while domain adaptation methods rely on abundant unlabeled data; consequently, neither approach effectively addresses the combined problem of cross-domain and few-shot learning. To address this issue, a two-stage framework named class-specific adapters and segment anything model (SAM) refinement Network is proposed for cross-domain few-shot IR ship semantic segmentation. The framework first learns domain-invariant features and then leverages a handful of annotated IR samples for class-specific adaptation and mask refinement. Specifically, in the first stage, a dual-branch network integrating wavelet transform and convolution is designed to extract robust cross-domain features, employing a random convolution perturbation strategy to stabilize adversarial training. In the second stage, independent, lightweight class-specific adapters are introduced for each target class and efficiently fine-tuned via self-supervised contrastive learning. Furthermore, the SAM is incorporated during inference through a prototype-driven prompt generation mechanism to refine initial segmentation results and enhance boundary accuracy. Experimental results on the VI-Ship and Agriculture-Vision datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across multiple evaluation metrics. Notably, under the extreme one-shot setting on the VI-Ship dataset, the proposed method achieves a mean Intersection over Union of 63.19%, exceeding the second-best method by 4.43%, thereby validating its effectiveness.
author: Ting Zhang, Pengyi Liu, Zhaoying Liu, Yingchun Chen, Hisham Alfuhaid, Mohammad Al-Antary, Muhammad Waqas, Syed Mudassir Hussain

title: Glacier Monitoring Using a Dual-Receiver Radar Architecture: Test Case of the Debris-Covered Forca Glacier
content: Monitoring glaciers is crucial for freshwater availability, climate regulation, and hazard assessment. However, investigating the internal structure of debris-covered glaciers poses significant challenges, as the heterogeneous nature of the propagation medium compromises the reliability of traditional monitoring techniques that rely on a-priori wave speed assumptions. In this article, we present an innovative radar technique based on a dual-receiver architecture capable of simultaneously retrieving the wave speed and the depth of the targets. The architecture was experimentally tested during field campaigns on the Forca Glacier (Italian Alps) in both winter and summer of 2024. Analysis of the collected data revealed a supraglacial debris layer thickness of 4.5 ± 0.4 m (with a relative permittivity of 49.2 ± 3.3) and an underlying ice thickness of 38.6 ± 2.8 m. This result was validated by a third experimental campaign performed in spring 2025 using passive seismic sounding, which yielded an independent ice thickness estimate of 34.3 ± 2.3 m. The consistency between electromagnetic and seismic independent estimations validates the dual-receiver architecture, demonstrating its potential for deriving the stratigraphy of complex debris-covered glaciers without a-priori assumptions.
author: Paolo Perret, Martina Lodigiani, Lorenzo Silvestri, Fabrizio Troilo, Francesco Zucca, Marco Pasian

title: A Novel Deep Learning Approach for Interferometric Phase Linking in Distributed Scatterer Interferometry
content: Distributed scatterer interferometry (DSI) has emerged as a widely adopted interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) processing strategy due to its ability to effectively enhance the phase quality of distributed scatterers while preserving spatial resolution. However, the phase linking (PL) step in DSI remains computationally intensive, posing challenges for large-scale applications. In this study, we propose a novel deep learning-based PL approach that effectively integrates spatiotemporal information from time-series interferograms to achieve rapid and robust phase optimization. The deep learning model was trained on real interferogram sequences and validated in low-coherence environments. These test regions included mixed areas of cropland, bare soil, and scattered buildings, a mine site, and grasslands. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves an average phase residue reduction of 95.98% and increases valid monitoring points to 179.44% of baseline values. Crucially, it requires only 2.04%–4.24% of the runtime and 22.58%–27.62% of the memory of conventional PL method. These improvements position our model as a computationally efficient and scalable tool, providing a practical approach for achieving enhanced phase quality in InSAR applications.
author: Chunjing Chen, Shouyi Chen, Feng Xiang, Chisheng Wang, Chuanhua Zhu

title: Adaptive Endmember Bundle Extraction With a Modified Differential Evolution Algorithm for Addressing Spectral Variability in Geology-1 Hyperspectral Imagery
content: High-precision extraction of alteration mineral information provides crucial guidance for hyperspectral remote sensing mineral exploration. Currently, widely used hyperspectral datasets (such as ZY-1 02D and GF-5) lack targeted design in spectral coverage and sensitive bands for minerals. The successful launch of China's first dedicated hyperspectral satellite for geology and mineral exploration—Geology-1–has addressed this gap and provides an optimal data source for detailed alteration mineral identification. However, mineral spectra are susceptible to variation under complex geological conditions. To effectively characterize this spectral variability, the endmember bundle method is adopted, which depicts an endmember category through a set of spectra. Existing endmember bundle extraction methods suffer from low computational efficiency and inadequate accuracy when processing high-dimensional hyperspectral data under complex geological conditions, which limits the effective interpretation of mineral information. To address this challenge, we propose an Adaptive Differential Evolution Endmember Bundle Extraction method (ADE-EBE). Based on a multistage optimization framework, this method transforms high-dimensional discrete space into low-dimensional continuous space for dynamic optimization. By employing an adaptive differential evolution algorithm to globally search for optimal endmember combinations, it significantly enhances the stability and accuracy of endmember bundle extraction. Experiments on simulated dataset and Geology-1 hyperspectral imagery show that ADE-EBE achieves superior overall performance compared to traditional and state-of-the-art methods in terms of spectral fidelity, abundance accuracy, and computational efficiency.
author: Linpei Yang, Ruyi Feng, Banxiao Ruan, Kaijun Yang

title: MSM_RoadNet: Multispectral Deep Network for Road Extraction With Application to Temporal Change Detection
content: With its abundant spectral information, multispectral remote sensing has become a pivotal tool for the intelligent management of road infrastructure, specifically in the precise extraction and dynamic monitoring of road networks. However, this field still faces three major challenges: the lag in multispectral data supply (with most applications still relying on single-source RGB imagery), the insufficient fusion of multispectral features and topology modeling in traditional models, and the disconnection between segmentation and detection functionalities. The work constructed a dedicated multispectral road dataset (MCRData) based on Sentinel-2 imagery to compensate for the limited spectral representation of traditional RGB data.
