Experiments on public nighttime tracking benchmarks, including NAT2021, NAT2021L, DarkTrack2021, and UAVDark135, show that the proposed algorithm outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, thus verifying the effectiveness and robustness of SFSTrack for UAV object tracking in nighttime low-light conditions.
author: Lifan Sun, Yuxuan Du, Yang Liu, Dongkai Zhang, Dan Gao

title: Scattering Characterization and Correction Method of Spaceborne GNSS-R for Wind Speed Retrieval in Coastal Regions
content: Coastal wind speed retrieval from Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) is affected by coastal sea-state changes that alter the relationship between wind speed and the normalized bistatic radar cross section (NBRCS). Finite depth, fetch-limited wave growth, and enhanced coherent scattering can modify sea surface roughness and introduce systematic differences between coastal and open-ocean observations. These effects limit the use of spaceborne GNSS-R in regions where high-resolution wind information is needed. This study characterizes the scattering behavior of spaceborne GNSS-R observations in coastal regions and develops a correction method for coastal wind speed retrieval. A coastal scattering model is constructed by incorporating a finite-depth modification of the wave spectrum, an empirical fetch-limited growth relationship, and a coherent scattering component. The model is used to interpret depth- and distance-dependent NBRCS variations and to build theoretical and empirical lookup tables (LUTs) for coastal NBRCS correction. Validation with Cyclone Global Navigation Satellite System (CYGNSS) observations supports the corrected simulation, with an NBRCS bias of about 0.8 dB and a root-mean-square error (RMSE) below 4 dB within 200 km of the coast, together with a 0.9% reduction in the RMSE of the simulated delay–Doppler map (DDM) power distribution within 50 km. When the LUTs are combined with the GMF method, both corrections improve coastal wind retrieval performance for winds above 4 m/s. The empirical LUT changes the bias from −1.04 to 0.40 m/s, whereas the theoretical LUT changes it to −0.93 m/s. The empirical LUT provides a stronger reduction of systematic wind speed underestimation, whereas the theoretical LUT gives a smaller but physically interpretable correction with more stable RMSE. This advancement not only reduces coastal simulation biases but also expands the usable coverage of GNSS-R wind products into previously excluded coastal environments.
author: Weichen Sun, Dongkai Yang, Feng Wang, Xiangchao Ma, Chuanrui Tan, Jiaorong Chen

title: Derivation of Aerial Insect Concentration With Triple Frequency Cloud Radar Observations
content: Aerial insects are vital for nature and society. Though methods to observe flying insects have consistently improved in the last few decades, insects remain difficult to monitor systematically and consistently over large spatial and temporal scales. Remote sensing with radars has proved to be one of the more effective tools for observation. However, as radars are most sensitive to targets that are of a similar size to the radar wavelength, the detectable subgroup of aerial insects of a certain size range depends on the employed radar. Here, we present a novel method based on spectral data of zenith-pointing Doppler cloud radars to estimate insect concentration in a vertical profile. Multiple meteorological state-of-the-art algorithms are combined to extract insect signals from the radar data and quantify their abundance from 150 to 3000 m above the ground. For evaluation, this method is applied to triple frequency data from X-band ( $\lambda =3.2$  cm), Ka-band ( $\lambda =0.85$  cm), and W-band ( $\lambda =0.32$  cm) Doppler cloud radars from a three-month summertime observation period in Germany. Observations of a case study of two elevated aerial insect layers are presented. Furthermore, differences in diel cycles of aerial insect concentration ( $aic$ ) obtained from the three radar instruments are compared. With its superior sensitivity to very small insects like aphids, W-band shows the highest  $aic$  values during daytime in the lower altitudes. Generally, the higher wavelength radars show higher  $aic$  in higher altitudes (Ka-band) and during the night (X-band).
author: Moritz Lochmann, Birgen Haest, Teresa Vogl, Roel van Klink, Freya I. Addison, Maximilian Maahn, Willi Schimmel, Christian Wirth, Johannes Quaas, Heike Kalesse-Los

title: Toward Quasi-Global Gap-Free Ocean Wind Speed Mapping Enabled by Rapid-Sampling GNSS Reflectometry Satellites
content: Accurate and continuous global ocean wind data are crucial for understanding weather and climate patterns. However, gaps in observations often limit the availability of measured ocean wind maps. As an emerging remote sensing technique, spaceborne global navigation satellite system reflectometry (GNSS-R) platforms have been providing billions of ocean surface observations with a revisit period shorter than one day, leading to the unprecedented potential of improving temporal and spatial resolutions simultaneously. In this study, we propose an optimal interpolation (OI) scheme to generate quasi-global gap-free ocean wind maps using rapid-revisit GNSS-R observations. NOAA Cyclone GNSS (CYGNSS) version 1.2 (V1.2) wind products are resampled to a 6-h temporal resolution and a  $0.25^{\circ } {\,}\times {\,}0.25^{\circ } $  spatial grid. A space–time OI based on isotropic Gaussian fields is then applied to assign weights to measurements and fill in each target grid. The averaged root-mean-squared errors (RMSEs) of OI reconstructed winds are 1.31, 1.32, and 1.76 m/s, compared to European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) ERA5, Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer-2 (AMSR2) all-weather, and Soil Moisture Active Passive (SMAP) final wind products, respectively. The reconstructed fields demonstrate stable spatiotemporal performances relative to these reference datasets. Case studies of Super Typhoon Surigae and Typhoon IN-FA further illustrate the method’s capability, showing improved detection of peak intensity on time compared to ECMWF ERA5 winds and consistent alignment of detected typhoon centers with best track data. Triple collocation is applied for computing the uncertainty of OI reconstructed winds (0.98 m/s on average), and the interpolation error of the proposed OI scheme is about 1.08 m/s according to the OI reconstructed wind fields with the input of track-masked ERA5 winds. This work provides a valuable approach for improving the continuity and reliability of GNSS-R ocean wind products, offering potential benefits for weather forecasting, climate studies, and extreme event monitoring.
author: Hao Du, Ronan Fablet, Thi Thuy Nga Nguyen, Weiqiang Li, Estel Cardellach, Bertrand Chapron

title: A Multifactor Harmonized Vegetation Index Compositing (MHVC) Approach for Monitoring Vegetation Dynamics in Sandy Land Ecosystems With Landsat-8/9 and Sentinel-2
content: Accurate monitoring of vegetation dynamics in optically complex sandy land ecosystems requires high-spatiotemporal-resolution time series, yet generating such records from individual sensors remains fundamentally constrained by cloud contamination, dust interference, and temporal gaps. However, harmonizing Landsat-8/9 and Sentinel-2 data remains challenging because of cross-sensor spectral inconsistencies, phenological biases, and severe over-masking of valid observations by sand dust and thin clouds, issues that are particularly acute in sandy land ecosystems. Although the harmonized Landsat-Sentinel (HLS) v2.0 product incorporates bidirectional reflectance distribution function (BRDF) normalization and bandpass adjustment, it still exhibits substantial bias in sandy environments (RMSE = 0.14) due to high surface albedo, sparse vegetation, and frequent dust aerosols that amplify anisotropic reflectance and mixed-pixel effects. To address these limitations, we developed the multifactor harmonized vegetation index compositing (MHVC) framework and applied it to the Hulunbuir sandy land from 2017 to 2024. MHVC incorporates three methodological advances: 1) an XGBoost-based spectral harmonization model that removes systematic cross-sensor biases ( $R^{2} = 0.94$ ,  $RMSE = 0.03$ ); 2) a distance-probability joint scoring mechanism that replaces fixed geometric cloud penalties with confidence-modulated weighting, substantially increasing valid pixel retention under dust- and aerosol-prone conditions; and 3) an optimized 40-day compositing window combined with Savitzky–Golay (S-G) filtering for temporally continuous trajectory reconstruction. The framework enables bidirectional gap-filling between sensors with symmetric radiometric fidelity ( $R^{2} = 0.915$ ,  $RMSE = 0.031$ ). Validation against high-quality reference observations demonstrated strong spectral consistency ( $R^{2} = 0.903$ ,  $RMSE = 0.049$ ,  $Bias = 0.001$ ) and enhanced temporal stability, with the median temporal roughness index (TRI) reduced from 0.20 (raw observations) to 0.06 (MHVC), outperforming the multifactor weighted (MFW) baseline (median TRI  $\approx 0.08$ ). Independent validation against MODIS MOD13Q1 further confirms phenological consistency between MHVC and the coarser-resolution benchmark ( $r = 0.888$ ), with residual discrepancies attributable to the inherent scale difference between 30- and 250-m products rather than to framework errors. Cross-regional application to the Horqin, Hunshandak, and Mu Us sandy lands yield consistently high spectral fidelity ( $R^{2} = 0.882$ –0.935) and temporal stability (median TRI reduction confirmed in three of four regions), confirming the framework’s transferability without region-specific recalibration. Overall, MHVC provides a robust, phenologically faithful solution for generating multiyear vegetation time series in sandy land ecosystems, offering direct applicability to desertification monitoring and ecological management in optically challenging drylands across northern China and a broader potential for vegetation dynamics studies across global sandy lands, shrublands, and other sparsely vegetated regions where optical complexity limits conventional time-series approaches.
author: Rihe Gao, Quan Lai, Xinyi Liu, Haitao Lang, Wenjing Li, Qin Na

title: Physics-Informed CNN–LSTM Framework for Electromagnetic Inverse Scattering in Multilayer Soil With Surface Roughness
content: Accurate estimation of the electromagnetic (EM) properties of multilayer soil is essential for noninvasive environmental and geophysical monitoring but remains challenging due to surface roughness, subsurface heterogeneity, and the nonlinear, ill-posed nature of the inverse scattering problem. This work proposes a hybrid physics-informed deep learning framework for estimating layer-wise permittivity and conductivity from free-space reflection measurements in the L-band (1–2 GHz). The model integrates a convolutional neural network (CNN) for local spectral feature extraction and a long short-term memory (LSTM) network for modeling sequential dependencies across the frequency response, together with a physics-informed loss function that promotes EM consistency during training. The physics-informed module is implemented as a differentiable multilayer EM forward solver that embeds the governing reflection equations into the training process. A computationally efficient synthetic data pipeline is developed to generate realistic six-layer soil configurations, including random Gaussian surface roughness and randomly distributed inclusions. Full-wave simulations are used to compute reflection coefficients in TM mode, and engineered input channels capture both spectral and physically meaningful features. The proposed framework is evaluated against multiple baselines, including random forest regression, extreme gradient-boosting (XGBoost) regression, multilayer perceptron (MLP), CNN-only, and LSTM-only models. The proposed model achieves a test loss of 0.0152 and a normalized mean absolute error (MAE) of 0.0668 and provides the lowest average layer-wise normalized root-mean-square error (NRMSE) for both permittivity and conductivity among the evaluated models. The results demonstrate the potential of the proposed framework for noninvasive soil characterization in complex subsurface environments while also indicating that deep-layer conductivity retrieval remains more challenging due to reduced sensitivity and limited information content in the measured reflection response.
author: Sahar Bagherkhani, Franco De Flaviis

title: TBN: An All-in-One Triplet Branch Network for Adverse Weather Cross-View Geo-Localization
content: Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) aims to match images of the same location captured from different viewpoints, such as those taken by drones and satellites. However, most existing approaches primarily focus on learning discriminative descriptors under general conditions using carefully tailored architectures, while paying insufficient attention to weather-induced image degradation. As a result, their performance deteriorates significantly under adverse environmental conditions. Revisiting these limitations of existing approaches, this article focuses on various adverse weather scenarios and capitalizes on the intrinsic commonalities among them to propose a unified all-in-one ConvNeXt-based CVGL framework called the triplet branch network (TBN). Specifically, we design a global information purifier (GIP) and a local information purifier (LIP) to handle occlusions caused by uneven degradation patterns. Additionally, a style distribution coordinator (SDC) is introduced to mitigate style shifts due to aerosol effects and reduce the cross-domain gap, thereby facilitating more robust, domain-invariant representation learning through multiscale feature fusion (MSF). Our approach demonstrates advanced performance in drone-view target localization and drone navigation tasks, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in evaluations on the University-1652 and SUES-200 datasets under adverse weather simulation settings. Our code is available at https://github.com/Moish25/TBN.
author: Yu Meng, Maohuan Wang, Lei Sun

title: Improving Temperature and Emissivity Separation Algorithm for Thermal Infrared Hyperspectral Remote Sensing: Considering Ozone Emission Effects
content: In thermal infrared hyperspectral remote sensing, accurate retrieval of land surface temperature (LST) and land surface emissivity (LSE) is fundamental to quantitative applications. Most existing temperature and emissivity separation (TES) algorithms primarily focus on noise suppression, adaptation to specific surface types, or improvements in computational efficiency, while relatively limited attention has been paid to retrieval disturbances caused by ozone emission. To address this issue, this study proposes the ozone-weighted TES (OWTES) algorithm, whose core concept is to enhance the weighting of ozone-sensitive spectral bands within the cost function. By strengthening the error constraints in this spectral region during the inversion process, OWTES effectively mitigates the adverse impact of noise on the retrieval procedure. Experimental results demonstrate that OWTES achieves higher retrieval accuracy for both LST and LSE, with particularly significant advantages under low-temperature and high-noise conditions. Compared with several representative TES algorithms, OWTES improves LST retrieval accuracy by an average of approximately 0.75 K and enhances LSE retrieval accuracy by about 58%. In addition, the algorithm maintains the lowest error level when validated using measured emissivity data, demonstrating high stability and reliability. These results indicate that OWTES can effectively preserve surface spectral characteristics and improve TES retrieval accuracy under complex observation conditions. Overall, the proposed OWTES method provides a more stable and reliable solution for TES in thermal infrared hyperspectral remote sensing.
author: Xia Zhang, Ruohan Chen, Chunlai Li, Guofei Shang, Zhe Li, Yongli Chen, Haoran Xiong, Suya Zhao, Chengyu Liu

title: First View of Synchronous Wave and Wind Retrieval From 1mC-SAR TOPW Image During Typhoons
content: Since 2024, China’s 1mC synthetic aperture radar (1mC-SAR) constellation, operating in terrain observation by progressive scans (TOPSs) mode (40-m resolution and 600-km swath), has enabled daily typhoon observations over the western North Pacific. This study represents the first simultaneous retrieval of typhoon wind and wave parameters from 1mC-SAR observations. Unlike conventional methods relying on a low-signal-to-noise-ratio (SNR) vertical-horizontal (VH) polarized normalized radar cross section (NRCS), we develop a Transformer-based joint wind–wave retrieval framework for typhoon conditions. A dataset of over 800 000 subscenes from 208 images of 28 typhoons during 2024–2025 was collocated with the reconstructed wind speed and regional ocean modeling system–simulating WAves nearshore (ROMS–SWAN) hindcast wave fields. The Transformer uses a total of 19 image-derived intensity and spectral features to retrieve significant wave height (SWH) and dominant wave period (DWP). For wind speed retrieval, the retrieved wave parameters are used as auxiliary inputs, and wind–wave growth constraints are embedded into the loss function. The Transformer outperforms a multilayer perceptron (MLP), a residual network (ResNet), and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost), achieving improved retrieval accuracy for SWH (RMSE = 0.60 m,  $r = -0.96$ ), DWP (RMSE = 1.23 s,  $r = 0.79$ ), and wind speed against soil moisture active passive (SMAP) observations (RMSE = 2.50 m/s,  $r = 0.92$ ). Translation-induced asymmetry analysis shows strong wind–wave coupling over the left and back flanks of Northern Hemisphere typhoons ( $r = 0.46$ –0.71), whereas the right flank is weakly coupled with the back flank ( $r = 0.04$ –0.47). These results indicate that typhoon-generated waves exhibit more pronounced asymmetry than the wind field. Future work will focus on direct deep-learning-based retrieval of typhoon wave spectra from SAR imagery, which may further improve the understanding of complex sea states and wave propagation under extreme tropical cyclone (TC) conditions.
author: Weizeng Shao, Yuyi Hu, Qingping Zou, Lei Zhou, Qingjun Zhang, Xingwei Jiang

title: Refocusing of Moving Ships in Spaceborne SAR Imagery via Space-Variant Phase Compensation
content: Due to the influence of sea waves, winds, and other complex environmental effects, maritime ships often suffer from severe defocusing in high-resolution synthetic aperture radar (SAR) images. The motions of the ship typically exhibit distinct space-variant features, which pose great challenges in ship imaging. To correct this spatial variance, this article proposes a space-variant phase compensation (SVPC) method based on the analysis of composite motion coupling between translational and rotational motions. Specifically, after the image segmentation, we first present a fast iterative algorithm based on monotonic iterative minimum entropy (MIME) to compensate for the azimuth-invariant phase terms. It applies an optimized transformation technique to achieve rapid indirect descent of the objective function. Then, we propose an azimuth-variant phase compensation algorithm based on the nonlinear chirp scaling (NCS) principle. It suppresses azimuthal variance through the perturbation function and modified Doppler compensation and, finally, utilizes a consistent compression filter to refocus the azimuth signal. Ultimately, experiments on both simulated and real SAR data demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method, outperforming other comparison techniques.
author: Jia Dong, Peng Liu, Ya-Qiu Jin

title: iHL-SC: A High-Likelihood-Constrained Spatial-Correlation Framework for Continuous GEOSC-SAR Imaging of Sea-Surface Waves
content: This study addresses continuous geosynchronous orbit (GEO) synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging through the formation of a temporally ordered, motion-compensated SAR-observable wave-pattern intensity image sequence over a selected beam-accessible ocean scene. The ultralong aperture integration amplifies motion-induced phase errors and azimuth decorrelation. GEO spatial-correlation SAR (GEOSC-SAR) based on model-based optimal 2-D spatial correlation (MBO2D-SC) enables single-frame focused imaging by optimizing blockwise motion parameters and synthesizing a motion-dependent spatial-correlation kernel for phase compensation. In continuous imaging of sea-surface waves, however, frame-to-frame motion evolution makes the previously optimized kernel mismatched for subsequent frames, leading to residual phase errors, spurious correlation responses, and temporal defocusing. Reoptimizing from a broad global domain at every frame avoids reuse-induced mismatch but incurs substantial computational cost and unstable convergence in a highly nonconvex landscape. We propose interframe high-likelihood spatial-correlation (iHL-SC) processing, a high-likelihood-constrained framework that converts framewise cold-start optimization into a closed-loop procedure in a time-variant blockwise first-order 2-D (TV-BFOTD) parameter space. iHL-SC represents interframe uncertainty using an evolving genetic-algorithm (GA) particle-set distribution and predicts a center–radius prior to construct a high-likelihood admissible region for each incoming frame. GA sampling and evolution are performed directly within this region, while any out-of-bound offspring are deterministically repaired via box projection to enforce hard-bound feasibility before signal-level evaluation. Based on the projected feasible state, we further develop MBO2D-HL-SC, which synthesizes a likelihood-conditioned spatial-correlation kernel to suppress mismatch-driven spurious responses. Beyond a Kalman-filter baseline, we develop PointGRU-Former to infer compact yet robust motion priors from historical particle-group evolution by combining Transformer-based global temporal modeling with PointGRU-based local aggregation. Experiments demonstrate improved multiframe focusing quality and processing efficiency with iHL-SC under the adopted geometry and controlled simulation settings and show that PointGRU-Former provides more accurate priors than competing predictors within the same iHL-SC pipeline.
author: Yuhang Li, Mei Liu, Xuemei Sui

title: An Iterative Change Detection Method Based on Robust PCA for Small SAR Image Datasets
content: This article introduces an iterative change detection (CD) method for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery based on robust low-rank and sparse matrix decomposition, specifically robust principal component analysis (RPCA). Instead of using fixed pairs of reference and surveillance images, as are typically used in conventional RPCA-based CD methods, our method progressively integrates multiple reference images and adaptively updates the regularization parameter at each iteration. This iterative scheme improves the separation between static clutter and sparse changes, significantly reducing the false alarm rate (FAR). The proposed iterative CD method is also explored using an extension of RPCA to tensors, known as tensor robust principal component analysis (TRPCA), thereby enabling its application to multitemporal and multidimensional SAR datasets. The experiments were conducted using real SAR data from the Swedish CARABAS-II system. The results showed that our iterative scheme outperforms noniterative CD methods based on a pair of SAR images and achieves competitive results when compared to CD methods using SAR image stacks, indicating that the iterative approach can be an effective solution in scenarios where SAR image availability is limited and SAR acquisitions are expensive.
author: Lucas P. Ramos, Dimas I. Alves, Leonardo T. Duarte, Renato Machado, Viet T. Vu, Mats I. Pettersson

title: Estimating Tropospheric Delay in Spotlight Mode InSAR Data Using Multisquint Processing
content: Tropospheric phase artifacts remain one of the largest sources of noise in interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR) data. With current observation geometries, the atmospheric delay signals and ground displacements are not easily separable, thus limiting the accuracy of geophysical retrievals even with the most sophisticated postprocessing atmospheric methods. A proposed multisquint observation strategy addresses this limitation with multiple observations of the same target obtained from at least three distinct squint angles to concurrently solve for the atmosphere as well as horizontal and vertical deformation vectors. Here, we present the results of a feasibility study to evaluate the performance of a proposed spaceborne multisquint architecture that leverages this concept as part of NASA’s surface deformation and change (SDC) mission study. We used long dwell spotlight data from Capella Space and ALOS-2 and computed subapertures to obtain multisquint observations. Contrary to previous assumptions, we found that the troposphere is nonuniform across the period of observation, which requires an expansion of the inversion, such that a minimum of four squint angles is needed to account for nonuniformity and fully resolve tropospheric delay, horizontal deformation, and vertical deformation. Also contrary to previous studies, we found that observations from small ( $\lt \!\!10^{\circ } $ ) multisquint angle ranges can be used to successfully solve for delay and deformation as long as regularization is implemented to stabilize the inversion. Overall, our feasibility study suggests that a carefully designed spaceborne multisquint architecture can be used to concurrently solve for deformation and tropospheric delay to yield new science findings, with limitations given satellite characteristics, such as squint range and noise floor as well as target-specific characteristics.
author: Stacey A. Huang, Samuel F. Chan, Shadi Oveisgharan, Xiaoqing Wu, Néstor Yagüe-Martínez, Craig Stringham, Gordon Farquharson

title: A Near-Field Error Correction Method for Aperture Synthesis Radiometers Based on Spatial-Frequency-Domain Data
content: High-resolution passive microwave near-field imaging is of increasing importance for security screening, concealed object detection, and all-weather target imaging. However, in near-field conditions, the radiation signals received by a aperture synthesis radiometer (ASR) propagate as spherical waves rather than plane waves. This departure from far-field assumptions breaks the direct Fourier transform relationship between the visibility function and the target brightness temperature (TB) distribution, leading to severe blurring and artifacts in reconstructed images. To address this challenge, this article proposes a deep learning-based near-field error correction method, termed FDSF-Net. By learning the complex mapping between near-field and far-field frequency domains, the method compensates for near-field distortions directly in frequency domains, enabling high-quality imaging via conventional algorithms. Quantitative simulation results demonstrate that, compared to uncorrected near-field imaging, FDSF-Net reduces the root-mean-square error (RMSE) by 16.5%, improves the peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) by 8.6%, and enhances the SSIM by 4.2%. Furthermore, the proposed method shows certain robustness under observation distance variations, channel amplitude and phase errors, target position deviations, and brightness-temperature deviations. Validation on measured data shows that the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the corrected images is reduced by 30.8%, confirming the effectiveness of the method in restoring image details and suppressing background noise in practical applications.
author: Jian Dong, Jiaxin Li, Chengwang Xiao, Wenjing Wang, Haofeng Dou, Yi Ding, Mu Tian, Xin Li

title: Across-Track Tilt Concept for Sun Glint Mitigation in Future Earth-Observing Missions
content: Sun glint is a major limitation for spaceborne optical remote sensing of aquatic environments, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions. By dominating the at-sensor radiance, sun glint complicates atmospheric correction and reduces the quality of aquatic reflectance products derived across Earth observation missions. This study presents a machine learning framework to quantify sun glint probability and evaluates the across-track tilt observational concept as a potential sun glint mitigation approach for satellite remote sensing missions. More than 14,000 cloud-free Landsat 8 and 9 scenes were analyzed to quantify glint occurrence as a function of sun-sensor geometry, latitude, and season. Logistic regression and machine learning models identified solar zenith angle (SZA), wind speed, and viewing zenith angle (VZA) as the primary predictors of sun glint probability, achieving > 98% classification accuracy. Sun glint contamination was found to be negligible for SZA > 40° but frequent at SZA < 35°, particularly in forward-scattering geometries. Introducing a 3° across-track westward tilt reduces the proportion of glint-contaminated pixels by ~21% in tropical and subtropical regions, while larger tilts of 6°, 9°, and 12° yield reductions of 43%, 59%, and 74%, respectively. These findings underscore the value of incorporating glint mitigation strategies in future Earth observing mission designs. A modest across-track tilt, combined with post-acquisition glint detection and correction, offers a practical pathway to increasing the availability of high-quality aquatic observations for monitoring inland and coastal water quality and ecosystems.
author: Saeed Arab, Christopher J. Crawford, Benjamin Page, Kevin R. Turpie, Peter Gege, David R. Thompson, Kelly M. A. Luis

title: Conv-J2SMamba: A New Hybrid Convolutional and Joint Scan Merge Structured Mamba Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Recently, Mamba has shown great potential in long-sequence modeling and has begun to be applied to hyperspectral image classification (HSIC). However, Mamba exhibits relatively weak local feature modeling capability. Moreover, as a causal sequence model, Mamba can only obtain a global receptive field at the end of the sequence, which fundamentally limits its ability to capture the complex spatial structures of hyperspectral images (HSIs). To address this problem, this article proposes a new hybrid convolution and joint scan merge structured Mamba network (Conv-J2SMamba) for HSIC, designed to capture the complex spatial structures and local–global features of HSIs. Specifically, we employ a convolutional branch to extract local features and design a joint scan merge structured Mamba (J2SMamba) to deeply model HSI spatial structures and capture global contextual features. Within J2SMamba, a joint scan merge (JSM) strategy and a structure-aware state-space model (SASSM) are constructed. The JSM strategy concatenates and integrates dual HSIs, followed by joint scan and joint merge operations. Compared with conventional scanning methods, this strategy enables each pixel location to obtain a global omnidirectional receptive field while maintaining the original sequence length unchanged. SASSM further employs the structure-aware state fusion (SASF) equation to capture spatial structural dependencies in HSIs, significantly enhancing the flow and propagation of contextual information. To further integrate the generated features, a contrastive-guided feature fusion (CGFF) module is also introduced, which utilizes a multilevel attention mechanism to guide the key feature enhancement and multilevel feature fusion. Experimental results on four public HSIs datasets demonstrate that the proposed Conv-J2SMamba model outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
author: Peiyi Xie, Lianhui Liang, Shaoquan Zhang, Ying Zhang, Jun Li, Antonio Plaza

title: Scale-Aware Mamba-Driven Implicit Representation for Arbitrary-Resolution Hyperspectral Pan-Sharpening
content: Pan-sharpening aims to reconstruct high-resolution hyperspectral images (HR-HSI) by fusing the spectral information of hyperspectral images (HSI) with the spatial details provided by panchromatic (PAN) images. However, most existing pan-sharpening methods regard the fusion of hyperspectral and PAN images at different spatial resolutions as independent tasks, necessitating the training of a distinct model for each scale. This paradigm is impractical in real-world applications, as maintaining separate models for every spatial scale incurs prohibitive computational and storage costs in arbitrary-resolution pan-sharpening scenarios. Therefore, we propose the scale-aware Mamba-driven implicit representation network (SAMIR-Net), a unified scale-aware framework for arbitrary-resolution hyperspectral pan-sharpening. The proposed SAMIR-Net consists of two main components: scale-aware modality interactive extraction and implicit spatial–spectral reconstruction. The scale-aware modality interactive extraction is realized through a dual-branch, scale-sensitive subnetwork that collaboratively extracts scale-aware multimodal features and facilitates spatial–spectral interaction. The implicit spatial–spectral reconstruction module (IS2RM) further performs cross-attention between spatial and spectral embeddings, enabling flexible and robust coordinate-based pan-sharpening at arbitrary resolutions. Extensive experiments show that SAMIR-Net achieves favorable performance compared with representative state-of-the-art methods and exhibits improved generalization across different upsampling factors, indicating its potential for flexible hyperspectral reconstruction. The code is available at: https://github.com/Jiahuiqu/SAMIR-Net
author: Yi Tian, Jiahui Qu, Wenqian Dong, Yunsong Li

title: Local–Global Progressive Network for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Image Fusion
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) and multispectral image (MSI) fusion aims to generate high-spatial-resolution HSIs (HR-HSIs) by integrating the rich spectral information with the fine spatial details. However, this task remains challenging due to the heterogeneous discrepancies between HSI and MSI in both spatial resolution and spectral dimensionality. The interpolated HSI and MSI often exhibit inconsistent spatial details during feature interaction, complicating reliable cross-modal correspondence and effective exploitation of complementary information. Existing methods struggle to simultaneously suppress cross-modal interference, preserve local details, model global context, and integrate multilevel features. To address these challenges, we propose a local–global progressive network (LGP-Net). First, a spatial–spectral interactive filtering (SSIF) module is designed for early-stage input processing, which enhances discriminative features while suppressing noise and interference caused by dimensional disparities. Second, a local–global collaborative learning (LGCL) module is built to extract stable local details through a physics-guided convolutional branch (PGCB) and cross-modal global dependencies through a multihead cross-shift Transformer (MCST). Finally, a cyclic-refined progressive fusion (CRPF) module is proposed to integrate features through progressively expanding receptive fields and a cyclic accumulation mechanism, facilitating deep multilevel integration while mitigating inconsistencies. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that LGP-Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods across a range of quantitative metrics. To support reproducibility and future research, we would make the source code publicly available on GitHub.
author: Shuyu Zhang, Yixun Cai, Wangquan He, Sen Jia

title: Uncertainty-Aware Exchange Consistency Learning for Robust Semi-Supervised Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: To alleviate the reliance on large volumes of labeled remote sensing images (RSIs), semi-supervised learning has emerged as a promising paradigm for change detection (CD) applications. Due to the diversity of RSI acquisition conditions, pseudo-changes arising from radiometric discrepancies and seasonal variations can limit the applicability of CD methods in complex scenarios, especially in semi-supervised settings with abundant unlabeled data. However, existing semi-supervised CD (SSCD) methods often focus on the discrepancy between labeled and unlabeled samples rather than the internal diversity within bitemporal pairs, thus struggling to cope with these pseudo-change issues. In this work, we propose a robust consistency regularization framework for SSCD that establishes a unified and comprehensive perturbation space via intertemporal, intratemporal, and feature-level perturbations, improving network adaptability to complex scenarios. Specifically, a novel and effective data perturbation approach, SwapMix, is proposed to explicitly model cross-temporal discrepancies and to embed intertemporal perturbations tailored for SSCD tasks. Subsequently, we design an exchange consistency learning (ECL) strategy to enforce prediction consistency between the multibranch perturbed inputs for labeled and unlabeled data, encouraging the network to learn feature representations that are robust to radiometric inconsistencies and seasonal variations. To stabilize semi-supervised learning, we design an uncertainty-driven perturbation-specific modulation (UPSM) mechanism that quantifies the reliability of multiple consistency-learning streams and adaptively modulates the training process, thereby attenuating the influence of inaccurate supervisory signals. Consequently, our proposed uncertainty-aware ECL (UA-ECL) approach shows superior performance compared with state-of-the-art methods on the learning, vision, and remote sensing change detection (LEVIR-CD), season-varying change detection (SVCD), and Sun Yat-sen University change detection (SYSU-CD) datasets.
author: Hongyu Chen, Xiaozhu Yi, Shaoguang Huang, Hongyan Zhang

title: Toward Fine-Grained Remote Sensing Image–Text Retrieval via Hierarchical Multigranularity Alignment
content: Remote sensing text–image retrieval (RSTIR) has attracted increasing attention from researchers because of advancements in multimodal foundation models and the growing volume of remote sensing (RS) data. The mainstream approaches involve adapting pretrained models to the RS domain through transfer learning. However, existing methods often overlook fine-grained correspondences between images and textual descriptions, making it challenging to effectively align key regions in images with corresponding fine-grained textual semantics. We identify two primary limitations of mainstream methods: the inherent limitations of model capabilities and the scarcity of fine-grained datasets. To address these challenges, we propose an innovative fine-grained text–image retrieval (FTIR) method, along with a high-quality fine-grained dataset named RSITMD-FG. First, we design a region-query association module that enhances the model’s ability to perceive fine-grained semantics by constraining the alignment between key regions in images and keywords in textual queries during training. Second, we propose a multigranularity alignment strategy to mitigate the difficulty and noise sensitivity of fine-grained alignment. Our proposed alignment strategy transitions from coarse-grained context semantics to fine-grained word-level semantics. Finally, we construct the RSITMD-FG dataset that emphasizes important region features within images and includes more fine-grained textual queries. To the best of our knowledge, RSITMD-FG is currently the most fine-grained publicly available dataset for RSTIR. We validate the effectiveness of our method on different benchmarks. Without complex multimodal interactions or additional training data augmentation, our concise method achieves competitive performance, outperforming even some domain-specific models. Our code and dataset will be released at https://github.com/ZhangWeihang99/FTIR
author: Weihang Zhang, Wenkai Zhang, Jihao Li, Ruixue Zhou, Boyuan Tong, Chongyang Li, Jifei Fang, Xin Gao

title: Learning Where to Focus: Density-Driven Guidance for Detecting Dense Tiny Objects
content: High-resolution remote sensing imagery increasingly contains dense clusters of tiny objects, the detection of which is extremely challenging due to severe mutual occlusion and limited pixel footprints. Existing detection methods typically allocate computational resources uniformly, failing to adaptively focus on these density-concentrated regions, which hinders feature learning effectiveness. To address these limitations, we propose the dense region mining network (DRMNet), which leverages density maps as explicit spatial priors to guide adaptive feature learning. First, we design a density generation branch (DGB) to model object distribution patterns, providing quantifiable priors that guide the network toward dense regions. Second, to address the computational bottleneck of global attention, our dense area focusing module (DAFM) uses these density maps to identify and focus on dense areas, enabling efficient local–global feature interaction. Finally, to mitigate feature degradation during hierarchical extraction, we introduce a dual filter fusion module (DFFM). It disentangles multiscale features into high- and low-frequency components using a discrete cosine transform and then performs density-guided cross-attention to enhance complementarity while suppressing background interference. Extensive experiments on the AI-TOD and DTOD datasets demonstrate that DRMNet surpasses state-of-the-art methods, particularly in complex scenarios with high object density and severe occlusion.
author: Zhicheng Zhao, Xuanang Fan, Lingma Sun, Chenglong Li, Jin Tang

title: Uncertainty-Guided Semisupervised Change Detection for Remote Sensing Images
content: Semisupervised change detection (CD) in remote sensing images faces a challenge wherein existing methods employ uniform processing strategies that fail to account for spatial heterogeneity in prediction difficulty, resulting in insufficient learning of difficult regions such as change boundaries and subtle variations that exhibit high uncertainty yet contain crucial discriminative information for accurate detection. To systematically address this limitation, this article proposes uncertainty-guided semisupervised CD (USCD), a novel framework that introduces a triple-guarantee mechanism for targeted learning reinforcement in difficult regions based on systematic uncertainty quantification. The framework comprises three synergistic components: uncertainty-aware protective augmentation (UAPA) employs window-based difficulty assessment to identify high-uncertainty regions and implements selective copy-paste augmentation that preserves critical information while maintaining data diversity; difficult region contrastive learning (DRCL) enhances feature discriminability in challenging areas through both local contrastive learning with anchor-based hard sample mining and global contrastive learning using prototype-based memory banks; and uncertainty-guided loss reweighting (UGLR) applies exponential adaptive weighting that strengthens supervision signals in high-uncertainty labeled regions while simultaneously suppressing noise propagation from unreliable pseudolabels in unlabeled data. Extensive experimental validation across six diverse benchmark datasets, including LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, CDD, S2Looking, SYSU-CD, and JL1-CD, demonstrates that USCD achieves state-of-the-art performance with limited labeled data, showing substantial improvements, particularly concentrated in high-uncertainty regions, robust generalization capability validated through cross-domain transfer experiments, and competitive computational efficiency comparable to existing methods. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/G124556/USCD
author: Yupeng Gao, Tianyu Li, Pengxian Yin, Yiwen Hu, Shu Chen, Wei Zhang

title: Water Body Mapping Over the Qinghai–Tibet Plateau Using Tianmu-1 Constellation GNSS-R Data
content: Monitoring surface water dynamics over the Qinghai–Tibet plateau (QTP) is crucial for quantifying hydrological and ecological responses to climate change and assessing the sustainability of downstream water resources. However, optical and radar remote sensing methods are limited by frequent cloud and snow cover, complex topography, and high albedo, resulting in reduced classification accuracy and insufficient spatiotemporal continuity. Recent studies have demonstrated the potential of global navigation satellite system reflectometry (GNSS-R) for surface water detection. This study develops a water body classification model tailored to the QTP, using GNSS-R data from the Tianmu-1 (TM-1) constellation and implemented with the light gradient boosting machine (LightGBM) algorithm. To address the severe class imbalance in water body classification, a hybrid strategy combining oversampling and class-weight adjustment is employed, with model performance further enhanced through hyperparameter optimization. The results show that the GNSS-R observations from the TM satellites exhibit markedly higher sensitivity to surface water than to nonwater surfaces, and the resulting water masks align well with reference datasets, achieving an overall accuracy of 0.99 and an  ${F}1$ -score of 0.74. The time series of water extents retrieved from June 2024 to November 2024 are in good agreement with the reference data, and the comparison of typical lake boundary contours further demonstrates the reliability of the classification model. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of using GNSS-R for long-term monitoring of surface water dynamics across the QTP.
author: Jiaxing He, Nanshan Zheng, Kegen Yu, Jieqing Yu

title: A Scattering-Angle-Based Spatiotemporal Filtering Strategy for Waveform Inversion
content: Full-waveform inversion (FWI) is a widely used method for high-resolution velocity modeling. However, when offsets are limited or low-frequency content is insufficient, seismic data are often dominated by reflections, and migration components tend to dominate the inversion gradient. This weakens the tomographic components needed for updating deep long-wavelength structures and increases the risk of convergence to local minima. To address this issue, we propose a scattering-angle-based spatiotemporal filtering strategy for FWI. In this method, scattering angles are estimated from local structural dips and wavefield propagation directions. During gradient computation, angle-domain filtering is applied to selectively retain gradient components within a target scattering-angle range, thereby improving the recovery of the low-wavenumber background velocity model. Unlike conventional wavenumber-domain approaches, the proposed strategy operates directly in the time–space domain and avoids the additional computational and storage cost associated with frequent Fourier transforms and related nonstationary filtering operations. Numerical experiments on the Overthrust and Marmousi models show that the proposed method improves background-velocity recovery and remains stable in noisy data cases. The models optimized by the proposed method provide more suitable initial models for subsequent conventional FWI and help improve its convergence behavior and final inversion results.
author: Yajing Peng, Hui Zhou, Qin Su, Hanming Chen, Bo Yu, Yuxin Fu, Han Yang, Weixin Yang, Wenze Cheng

title: SU-CFNet: Spectral Unmixing Guided Cross-Fusion Network for Hyperspectral and Multispectral Images
content: The core objective of hyperspectral and multispectral image fusion (HSI–MSI fusion) is to generate high-spatial-resolution HSIs by combining the abundant spectral information of low-resolution hyperspectral images with the clear spatial details of high-resolution multispectral images. However, deep-learning-based fusion methods frequently suffer from spatial and spectral distortions. To address this issue, this article proposes a cross-fusion network named SU-CFNet for HSI–MSI fusion by integrating spectral unmixing theory with the transformer architecture. Specifically, a dual-branch spectral unmixing module is designed to refine the representation of hyperspectral spectral features through fine-grained spectral decomposition and feature calibration. A multiscale spatial information enhancement module is presented to adaptively extract spatial details and structural information at various scales from MSIs via a pyramid hierarchical structure, overcoming the limitations of single-scale feature extraction. Furthermore, a spectral–spatial cross-fusion module is constructed to capture long-range dependencies between spectral and spatial features via a cross-modal feature interaction mechanism, enabling complementary fusion and collaborative enhancement of the two types of features. To verify the effectiveness of the proposed model, systematic comparative experiments are conducted between spectral unmixing–guided cross-fusion network and seven state-of-the-art fusion methods on both simulated and real datasets. Both quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves the best performance in two core metrics: spectral fidelity and spatial detail preservation, significantly outperforming the comparison methods. In addition, auxiliary land cover classification experiments confirm that the fused images produced by our model can effectively improve the accuracy and stability of land cover classification and realize precise discrimination of typical land cover categories, fully demonstrating the practical value and application potential of the proposed method.
author: Yujie Wu, Jiguang Dai, Zheng Ma, Yunqi Liang

title: Bridging the SMAP Era and the Presatellite Period: Long-Term Surface Soil Moisture Reconstruction in a Humid and Semihumid Monsoon Basin Since 1951
content: The Soil Moisture Active Passive Level 4 (SMAP L4) soil moisture product offers global, high-resolution estimates generated via selective, quality-controlled assimilation of L-band brightness temperature sources, but its limited historical records (since 2015) constrain the following long-term geoscience studies. Given this, we reconstruct a retrospective surface soil moisture dataset (1951–2016) for a humid and semihumid area (Huai River Basin, China) using a Random Forest (RF) approach within a multisource framework that integrates ERA5_Land meteorological inputs, SMAP ancillary data, and static geographic information. The reconstructed dataset shows strong agreement with SMAP L4 observations, in-situ measurements, and land surface model outputs, especially in the northern and central basin (correlation > 0.6, ubRMSE < 0.04 m3/m3). Triple Collocation results and direct comparisons mutually corroborate each other, both indicating that model performance declines in regions with higher average rainfall. The Shapley Additive Explanations (SHAP) method is further applied to quantify the relative importance of input variables. Feature importance analysis confirms that SHAP and RF produce generally consistent variable rankings. In drier areas, soil properties dominate soil moisture variability, while in wetter regions, atmospheric forcings prevail. SHAP further reveals that longwave radiation and clay contribute positively to surface soil moisture, whereas surface temperature exerts a negative effect. These regional differences highlight the climatic sensitivity of soil moisture to spatially uneven and seasonally concentrated precipitation in humid and semihumid monsoon regions.
author: Yiling Yao, Haishen Lü, Tianjie Zhao, Yonghua Zhu, Qimeng Wang, Haiting Xu, Pan Duan, Yinghao Fu, Jiaying Liu, Xiaoyi Wang

title: Sentinel 2-Based AI Bitemporal Anomaly Detection for Urban Wastewater Mapping and Impacts Assessment
content: In recent years, a range of environmental challenges associated with wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) has intensified, particularly in densely populated urban areas where large volumes of wastewater must be processed on a daily basis. Under such conditions, treatment facilities are frequently forced to operate at or beyond their designed capacities, potentially reducing treatment efficiency, limiting effective pollutant removal, and exacerbating negative environmental impacts on surrounding ecosystems. In this context, the present study is the first to demonstrate the effectiveness of an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven spectral anomaly detection framework for identifying atypical surface conditions related to wastewater discharge using multispectral, multitemporal Sentinel-2 (S2) imagery. The proposed methodology integrates spatial downscaling to strengthen the representation of subtle anomalous patterns, a deep spectral autoencoder for feature learning and anomaly detection, and geospatial postprocessing to ensure spatial coherence and result interpretability. A fully connected spectral autoencoder is trained to learn the intrinsic spectral manifold of selected seasonal scenes, with pixel-wise reconstruction errors subsequently used to generate anomaly maps. In addition, an unsupervised bitemporal anomaly detection approach is implemented, exploiting joint spectral, spatial, and temporal reconstruction errors to identify significant changes between multitemporal remote sensing images. Wastewater within the oxidation channels and clarifiers of the Choutrana 2 WWTP, and the Côte Nord as well, is clearly delineated in the anomaly maps and uniquely highlighted in the bitemporal anomaly map. The resulting anomaly maps are further compared with S2 Alpha residual and Log residual–based maps, as well as principal component analysis transformation-based maps. The strong convergence observed among these results highlights both the robustness of the proposed AI-based framework and the alarming severity of the environmental conditions in the study area.
author: Nouha Mezned, Belgacem Dkhala, Faten Alayet, Wafa Talhaoui

title: TSGE-Net: A Topology-Spectrum Guided Enhancement Network for SAR Target Classification
content: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) automatic target recognition is an important task in military reconnaissance and maritime monitoring. However, spatial-domain models struggle to preserve the geometric configuration of sparse and discontinuous SAR scattering centers. Frequency-domain analysis can reveal scattering variations that are less explicit in spatial features, but existing spatial-frequency aggregation often introduces frequency-domain responses without explicitly assessing their relevance to target-related scattering patterns, which may bring structure-irrelevant cues into the final representation. To address this challenge, we propose the topology-spectrum guided enhancement network (TSGE-Net). TSGE-Net consists of two key components, namely the structure-aware module (SAM) and the cross-domain feature fusion module (CFFM). Specifically, SAM organizes spatially discontinuous scattering responses into topology-aware representations. CFFM then performs cross-domain fusion by selectively incorporating frequency-domain cues that are consistent with the learned scattering organization. Extensive experiments on four public SAR datasets and a mixed-category dataset show that TSGE-Net consistently outperforms competitive baselines and maintains robust performance across diverse SAR classification settings.
author: Jiarui Li, Xutao Sun, Yang Li, Miao Fu, Xiangyang Wang, Yang Liu

title: A Signal Processing Method for Azimuth Multichannel SAR With Partial Channels Missing
content: The azimuth multichannel synthetic aperture radar (SAR) system enhances spatial sampling by increasing channels, resolving the conflict between high resolution and wide mapping swath. Future systems will integrate more channels for complex tasks like moving target detection. However, this increases the likelihood of at least one channel failure. Missing echo data from individual channels reduces the equivalent pulse repetition frequency (PRF), weakens spatial sampling uniformity, and complicates the calculation of interchannel phase errors using azimuth cross correlation methods, leading to severe azimuth artifacts. To address these issues, this article proposes: a channel phase error calibration method that combines spectral estimation with the azimuth cross-correlation and a signal reconstruction method based on infinity norm optimization that specifically suppresses the severest azimuth artifacts. This approach extends multichannel signal processing theory for SAR signal reconstruction from aliased signals. By estimating the missing channel’s partial signal using iterative adaptive spectral estimation and then calculating interchannel errors with the cross-correlation method, the scheme mitigates the reduced correlation between adjacent channels due to channel absence. Infinity norm optimization is used to design reconstruction filters that suppress the peak of ambiguity energy, effectively reducing the strongest azimuth artifacts. The new method’s performance in terms of the strongest azimuth artifacts suppression and AASR relative to PRF was analyzed and compared with other methods. Both simulation and real data experiments validated the superiority of the new scheme.
author: Zonglin Yang, Zhimin Zhang, Fanlong Meng, Huaitao Fan, Chen Zhen, Yongwei Zhang, Sixi Hou, Yihai Wei

title: GAN-Based PAN-to-RGB Image Translation for True-Color 3-D Texture Generation Using GaoFen-7 Stereo Imagery
content: Three-dimensional (3-D) model reconstruction technology based on satellite stereo views has been widely applied in fields, such as urban mapping and environmental simulation. However, due to resource constraints, typically only one view is equipped with both high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) and multispectral sensors, whereas the other view carries only a PAN sensor, as exemplified by the GaoFen-7 satellite. Consequently, the 3-D models reconstructed from such data cannot achieve full coverage with true-color textures. This limitation significantly constrains the utility of such models in advanced applications, including realistic 3-D visualization and semantic segmentation. Therefore, PAN-to-RGB images translation method is of critical importance for generating true-color 3-D textures. In this article, a generative adversarial network-based PAN-to-RGB model is proposed to establish a novel framework for true-color 3-D textures generation from satellite stereo data. Multiscale features and symmetric luminance color decoders are utilized to overcome color desaturation, inaccuracy, and distortion in conventional algorithms. By combining convolutional neural networks for local feature modeling and transformers for global feature modeling, this approach learns luminance and color mappings to produce high-resolution, high-fidelity RGB images in CIELAB space. We conducted experimental validation on Gaofen-7 satellite data, and the results demonstrated that the proposed algorithm effectively mitigates the issues of unsaturated, inaccurate, and distorted colors, achieving significant improvements in key metrics, such as Fréchet inception distance and $\boldsymbol {\Delta {CF}}$. Furthermore, this study marks the first successful implementation of city-scale true-color 3-D texture generation using Gaofen-7 satellite data, with notable visual improvements in building façades.
author: Xiaowei He, Yingzi Xiong, Xiao Ling, Qinghong Sheng, Bo Wang, Xiao Xu, Zhaocong Wu

title: Distinguishing Mineral Oil Slicks From Low-Wind Areas Using High-SNR Synthetic Aperture Radar
content: We propose a method for distinguishing oil slicks from radar-dark, oceanic low wind areas using single acquisitions of high-SNR airborne and spaceborne SAR imagery. Airborne data were acquired by DLR’s F-SAR X- and L-band SAR instrument during a controlled release, oil-on-water campaign, and by NASA’s unmanned aerial vehicle synthetic aperture radar (UAVSAR) L-band SAR instrument over the Aleutian Islands in Alaska and the Coal Oil Point seep field near Santa Barbara, California. The spaceborne imagery consists of five RADARSAT-2 C-band SAR scenes containing either low-wind zones or mineral and plant oil, three of which were acquired during controlled releases in the North Sea. A RADARSAT-2 scene containing grease ice is included to demonstrate the potential of the proposed method to discriminate between oil slick and other look-alike types. The proposed product, referred to as KS index maps, is based on the Kolmogorov–Smirnov test statistic and is used as a measure of separability between ensembles of pixels inside vs. outside a radar-dark area. We use the theoretical Polarimetric Two-Scale Model to interpret the physical basis on which our method distinguishes between radar-dark oil slicks and low-wind zones relative to nearby open water in higher wind areas. KS index values for oil slick are consistently between −1 and 0, and KS index values for low-wind zones (and grease ice) are consistently between 0 and +1. The method is suitable for near-real-time implementation to aid oil spill response.
author: Cornelius Quigley, A. Malin Johansson, Cathleen E. Jones

title: Sea Clutter Suppression Method Based on Pyramid Multiscale Residual Attention Generative Adversarial Network
content: Sea clutter significantly impacts radar maritime target detection. Existing methods still show limited clutter suppression performance, and their generalization ability requires further improvement. To address this issue, a sea clutter suppression method based on the pyramid multiscale residual attention generative adversarial network (PMS-RA GAN) is proposed. In the generator, an integrated residual attention mechanism is designed to highlight the target and effectively suppress the background clutter. Furthermore, a multiscale pyramid adaptive feature extraction module based on sea clutter characteristics is designed to generate high quality radar time–frequency images. In addition, a composite loss function is proposed to balance between image quality and detail performance. Experimental results on real-world datasets demonstrate that PMS-RA GAN outperforms the existing methods in sea clutter suppression, significantly improving target detection performance.
author: Xiaolin Du, Keyao Lai, Xiaolong Chen, Guolong Cui, Jibin Zheng, Jinwei Wang, Dawei Zhao, Shuang Yu, Xuekuan Shi

title: Neural Spatiotemporal Interpolation for Gap Filling of GNSS-R Soil Moisture Data
content: Spaceborne Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) provides L-band observations suitable for soil moisture retrieval and monitoring, but the resulting datasets are inherently sparse in space and time. Many applications, however, benefit from access to seamless, gap-free soil moisture datacubes. This study introduces Neural Spatiotemporal Interpolation (NSTI), a deep learning framework for gap filling of GNSS-R–derived soil moisture spatiotemporal datacubes. NSTI formulates gap filling as a regression problem in which missing values are estimated from known values in their spatiotemporal neighborhoods through a single, spatially transferrable neural network model. The framework is evaluated on CYGNSS-derived surface soil moisture aggregated at 36 km and 9 km resolutions. Across both resolutions, NSTI achieves gap-filling errors comparable to state-of-the-art approaches while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters. Validation against data from SMAP (ubRMSE 0.049 m$^{3}$/m$^{3}$) and ERA5-Land (ubRMSE 0.056 m$^{3}$/m$^{3}$) further shows that the gap-filled datacubes preserve agreement with external reference datasets, when compared to the original sparse product. Together, these results demonstrate that spatiotemporal soil moisture structure can be consolidated into a unified and scalable deep learning framework for gap filling of sparse GNSS-R data.
